title: Move blocking filesystem work out of async request handlers
description: Several async handlers call std::fs synchronously (read_dir/read_to_string/copy), which can block the Tokio runtime under load. Convert to tokio::fs or wrap in spawn_blocking. Examples: backend/crates/octo/src/api/main_chat_pi.rs::get_prompt_commands (read_dir/read_to_string), backend/crates/octo/src/api/handlers.rs::list_workspace_dirs (read_dir), list_project_templates (read_dir), find_project_logo (read_dir), copy_template_dir (read_dir/fs::copy).

title: Remove OpenCode harness code
description:
Remove all OpenCode-specific code from Octo. OpenCode was the original agent harness; Pi has fully replaced it. ~100 files, ~14k lines of dead/unused code across backend and frontend. Removal improves maintainability, reduces compile times, and eliminates confusion between legacy OpenCode abstractions and the live Pi/canonical protocol path.

Guiding principles:
- Phase by subsystem, each phase self-contained and buildable
- Rename shared types before deleting OpenCode-only code
- Dead code first, live code last
- remote-build check + bun run build + smoke test after each phase

Estimated effort: ~9 hours across 10 phases.
close_reason: Epic complete: 11544 lines removed, 114 files changed. Only 12 legitimate 'opencode' refs remain (filesystem paths, test data, legacy data dir).

Main Chat Architecture Overhaul: Session-Based Conversations
description:
# Main Chat Architecture Overhaul: Session-Based Conversations

Replace the current one-long-chat model with discrete session-based conversations that have explicit boundaries, proper context handoff, and multi-stage context management.

## Problem Statement

Current architecture has several issues:
1. **Compaction summaries not persisted** - code comments claim they are saved to main_chat.db but no implementation exists
2. **History context not injected** - assistant must manually call `mmry search`, nothing automatic
3. **Dual message storage divergence** - Octo SQLite vs Pi session files get out of sync
4. **No handoff extraction** - important state lost when sessions become stale
5. **Display vs LLM context diverge** - after compaction, user sees full history but LLM sees summary
6. **No efficient history search** - assistant cannot search its own conversation history across sessions

## Proposed Architecture

### Session Model
- Each conversation is a discrete **session** with clear start/end boundaries
- Sessions have explicit lifecycle: active → stale → archived
- User can start new session explicitly (`/new`) or via idle timeout
- Full transcript preserved for audit/display, summarized for context injection

### Context Injection (on session start)
1. **System prompt** - PERSONALITY.md, USER.md (unchanged)
2. **Previous session summary** - auto-generated handoff from last session
3. **Recent memories** - top N relevant mmry entries (configurable)
4. **Pending tasks** - any unfinished work from previous session

### Multi-Stage Context Management (inspired by clawdbot)
1. **Soft trim** (at ~30% context fill) - truncate large tool results to head+tail
2. **Hard clear** (at ~50% fill) - replace old tool results with `[Old tool result content cleared]`
3. **Memory flush** (before compaction) - give agent one turn to persist important context
4. **Compaction** (at limit) - LLM summarization, then new session with summary injected

### Session End / Handoff
When session ends (idle timeout, explicit `/new`, or manual close):
1. Extract key decisions, insights, pending tasks
2. Generate session summary
3. Save to history table as `Summary` type
4. Archive transcript (keep for audit)

### History Search
The assistant needs efficient access to its own conversation history:
1. **Semantic search** - embed session transcripts/summaries for similarity search
2. **Keyword/full-text search** - find specific terms, commands, file paths mentioned
3. **Temporal queries** - "what did we discuss yesterday", "last week's decisions"
4. **Filtered search** - by session, by topic tag, by message type (user/assistant/tool)

This could be exposed as:
- MCP tool: `chat_history_search(query, filters)`
- Or integrated into existing mmry with a `chat:` namespace

## Sub-Tasks

### Phase 1: Foundation
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Define session lifecycle states and transitions
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Create session metadata schema (extends current SessionEntry concept)
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Implement session storage (SQLite table for sessions, separate from messages)
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Add session boundary detection (idle timeout, explicit reset triggers)

### Phase 2: Context Management
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Implement soft trim for tool results (in-memory, not on disk)
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Implement hard clear with placeholder
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Add memory flush turn before compaction
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Hook into Pi compaction events to save summaries

### Phase 3: Context Injection
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Build context injection service
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Auto-inject previous session summary on new session
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Auto-inject relevant mmry entries (semantic search or recent)
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Inject pending tasks/handoff items

### Phase 4: History Search
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Design history search schema (embeddings table, FTS index)
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Implement embedding generation for session content
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Implement semantic search over history
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Implement full-text/keyword search
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Expose as MCP tool or integrate with mmry
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Add temporal query support (date ranges, relative time)

### Phase 5: UI/UX
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Add session list/history view
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Add "New Session" button/command
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Show session boundaries in chat view
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Session export/archive functionality
- [ ] **octo-xxx** History search UI in frontend

### Phase 6: Migration
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Migrate existing main_chat.db to new schema
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Handle in-flight sessions during upgrade
- [ ] **octo-xxx** Backfill embeddings for existing history

## References
- Clawdbot architecture: `~/byteowlz/external-repos/clawdbot/src/`
- Current Octo main chat: `~/byteowlz/octo/backend/crates/octo/src/main_chat/`
- Pi session handling: `~/byteowlz/octo/backend/crates/octo/src/pi/`
- mmry for embedding patterns: `~/byteowlz/mmry/`

## Success Criteria
- Sessions have clear boundaries visible to user
- Context window managed gracefully without losing important information
- Previous session context automatically available in new sessions
- No divergence between display history and LLM context
- Compaction summaries actually persisted and used
- Assistant can efficiently search its own conversation history across all sessions

UI shows collapsed/expandable tool calls. Research needed on optimal checkpoint intervals and summary strategies.

title: Add browser extension mode (Option A) - fork Playwriter
description:
Browser extension mode for controlling user's existing browser.

## Reference Implementation
- ../external-repos/playwriter - Fork this for Octo extension
- ../external-repos/clawdbot/src/browser/extension-relay.ts - CDP relay pattern

## Approach
Fork remorses/playwriter and adapt for Octo:
- Chrome extension connects to Octo backend via WebSocket
- User clicks extension icon to grant tab access
- Agent controls tabs using Playwright API
- User sees agent actions in their own browser

## Benefits over server browser
- Uses user's existing logins (Gmail, GitHub, etc.)
- User's ad blockers, password managers work
- Real-time collaboration (user watches agent)
- Can bypass automation detection (disconnect extension)
- Zero server resources for browser

## Key Changes to Playwriter
1. WebSocket endpoint: localhost:19988 → Octo backend URL
2. Add authentication (user token from Octo session)
3. Octo branding in extension popup
4. Integrate with credential vault hooks
5. Add connection status to Octo frontend

## Playwriter Architecture (../external-repos/playwriter)
- Extension background.js: WebSocket to relay server
- Relay server: Bridges extension CDP to Playwright
- Single 'execute' tool: Full Playwright API access
- Accessibility snapshots with visual labels

## Depends on
- octo-k8z1 (server browser) should be done first
- Unified MCP interface should work for both modes

## Related
- octo-k8z1: Server browser (Option B)
- octo-k8z1.13: User interaction handoff (applies to both)

title: Security/perf/idiomatic audit fixes
description: Bundle of findings from the comprehensive review; child issues are linked as blockers.

title: Radix UI ContextMenu infinite loop on session.updated events
description: Maximum update depth exceeded error occurs when session.updated WebSocket events trigger re-renders of session list items wrapped in ContextMenu. This is a known Radix UI bug (https://github.com/radix-ui/primitives/issues/3385). Current mitigations: debounced refresh (100ms) and startTransition for state updates. May need further fixes like memoizing session items or increasing debounce timeout.
close_reason: Fixed: Added startTransition to refreshChatHistory to prevent rapid re-renders

title: Live model selection is broken
close_reason: Fixed by adding textValue prop to SelectItem components with ProviderIcon. Radix UI Select couldn't properly extract text content from SelectItems with complex children (images + spans).

title: Text input field stays enlarged after sending long message

title: WebView: Backend proxy for localhost servers
description:
Create proxy endpoint for agent-spawned web servers.

## Endpoint
GET/POST /api/session/{id}/webview/proxy
Query params:
  - port: number (required, 1024-65535)
  - path: string (optional, default /)

## Implementation
- Validate port is in allowed range
- Proxy request to localhost:PORT on server
- Support WebSocket upgrade (for Vite HMR, etc.)
- Forward all HTTP methods
- Stream responses for large files

## Security
- Only allow localhost/127.0.0.1 targets
- Rate limit requests per session
- Max request body size
- Timeout for slow backends


title: Sessions disconnect unexpectedly
description:
Opencode chats: sessions disconnect unexpectedly/arbitrarily.

Expected: Session remains connected/active unless user ends it or network drops.
Actual: Disconnect occurs without an obvious trigger.

Evidence / logs (2026-01-14):
- POST /sessions/get-or-create-for-workspace: opencode spawned (PID 3638670) on port 41872; session resumed successfully; status=Running (200).
- Immediately after, GET /sessions triggers WARN: "Local processes for session 7d2378da-3ec6-4491-99a1-5e42ac9d99a9 are not running, marking as stopped" (5ms request).
- Session list returns count=109.
- Subsequent proxy POST to /session/{id}/code/.../prompt_async returns 204.

More evidence (event delivery):
- When subscribing to sessions, backend logs: "User usr_dev subscribed to session <id>" followed by repeated WARNs: "Failed to send event to user usr_dev connection 0..6". This suggests stale/broken user connections or event bus delivery failures, which could manifest as UI disconnects / missing realtime updates.

Hypotheses:
- Race/incorrect liveness check for local opencode processes (marked stopped right after spawn/resume).
- WebSocket/user connection management bug causing failed event dispatch (stale connections, dropped WS, incorrect connection indexing).

Needs data to confirm:
- Browser WS close codes/reasons when disconnect happens.
- Whether opencode PIDs actually exit (crash) vs liveness check mis-detects.
- Any opencode stdout/stderr around the timestamp.

Repro: send message in Main Chat, observe spinner with no content; switch to another tab (e.g. sessions) and back; response appears. Suspect WS deltas not attached to visible message state. Needs fix for all message types.

title: Chat sessions: zombie entries, wrong titles, empty responses after reattach
description:
## Summary
Chat history is corrupted: zombie sessions with no readable_id that cannot be deleted, new messages appear under another session's title, and reattach often yields empty responses. UI shows session.create storms; list_sessions appears to miss running sessions (likely alias mismatch).

## Environment
- Frontend: wismut:dev (Vite)
- Backend: http://archlinux:8080
- User: wismut
- Workspace: /home/wismut/byteowlz/octo

## Symptoms
- Zombie sessions appear in chat history without readable_id.
- These zombie sessions cannot be deleted from UI.
- New messages sometimes appear under another session's title.
- Reattach often yields empty responses; readable_id only appears after sending a message.
- WebSocket logs show repeated session.create/list_sessions/get_state bursts.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Open frontend and log in as wismut.
2. Create a new chat session and send a message.
3. Reload the page during or after streaming.
4. Observe session list and send another message.

## Expected
- Session list contains only valid sessions with readable_id.
- Deleting a session removes it from history and runner.
- Reattach shows correct session title and readable_id without sending a message.
- New messages stay attached to the selected session.

## Actual
- Zombie sessions without readable_id remain in history and cannot be deleted.
- Messages appear under another session’s title.
- Empty responses or missing streaming unless a new message is sent.

## Logs
- WS: multiple session.create and list_sessions bursts; list_sessions often misses the active session ID.
- useChat: agent.working toggles inconsistently.

## Notes
Likely issues:
- Session identity mismatch between Pi native ID vs Octo UUID.
- list_sessions missing alias; reattach path falls back to history, causing mis-association.
- Delete path only closes WS session; no hstry delete.
close_reason: list_sessions now returns resolved Pi session IDs to avoid reattach mismatches

title: MCP Apps Support - Interactive UI in Chat
description:
Add support for MCP Apps extension to render interactive HTML interfaces (dashboards, forms, visualizations) directly in the chat UI. This enables richer user interactions beyond text/images - file browsers, build output viewers, deployment config forms, live metrics dashboards, etc.

## Phases

### Phase 1: Frontend Host Support
- Integrate AppBridge or @mcp-ui/client for iframe rendering
- Detect tool results with _meta.ui.resourceUri
- Render MCP App iframes in chat messages with proper sandboxing
- Handle bidirectional tool call proxying (app -> host -> server)

### Phase 2: Built-in Octo Apps
- Workspace file browser app (interactive tree, previews)
- Build/test output viewer app (collapsible panels, filtering)
- Container status dashboard app (start/stop/logs buttons)

### Phase 3: User MCP Server Apps
- Allow connected MCP servers to return apps
- Security review and capability restrictions
- Documentation for MCP server authors

## Key Benefits
- Context preservation: UI lives inside conversation
- Bidirectional data flow: Apps can call tools, host pushes results
- Security: Sandboxed iframe isolation
- Integration: Leverages existing MCP tool patterns

## References
- Spec: https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/extensions/apps
- SDK: https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/ext-apps
- Examples: map-server, pdf-server, system-monitor-server, etc.

title: Note: octo-ssh-proxy socket path must be mounted or moved for sandbox access
Build octo-runner daemon for multi-user process isolation
description:
Build a standalone runner daemon that runs as each Linux user via systemd user services. The runner listens on a Unix socket and accepts RPC commands to spawn/manage processes (opencode, pi, etc.) without requiring sudo from the main octo process.

Components:
- backend/src/runner/ - runner protocol types and client library
- backend/src/bin/octo-runner.rs - the runner daemon binary
- systemd/octo-runner.service - systemd user service unit file

RPC commands:
- SpawnProcess: spawn a process with args, cwd, env
- SpawnRpcProcess: spawn process with stdin/stdout pipes (for Pi RPC)
- KillProcess: kill by pid
- GetStatus: check if process is running

This enables proper multi-user isolation without octo needing elevated privileges.
close_reason: Built standalone octo-runner daemon with RPC protocol, client library, and systemd service files. Ready for integration with LocalRuntime and MainChatPiService.

title: Tauri iOS: Native HTTP via reqwest for reliable networking
close_reason:
Implemented native HTTP commands using reqwest in Rust, with transparent fetch polyfill for frontend.

## Problem
iOS WKWebView has strict networking restrictions that prevent standard fetch() from reaching external HTTP servers.

## Solution
Added custom Tauri commands with reqwest (like opencode2go uses).

### Files Changed
1. src-tauri/Cargo.toml - Added reqwest, tokio, tokio-stream, futures
2. src-tauri/src/lib.rs - Added http_get/post/put/patch/delete, discover_servers, start_sse_stream
3. lib/tauri-fetch-polyfill.ts - Transparent window.fetch replacement for Tauri
4. src/main.tsx - Import polyfill first
5. lib/tauri-http.ts - TypeScript wrapper for direct use

## Why It Works
reqwest uses iOS URLSession natively, bypassing WKWebView restrictions.

## App Store
Fully compliant - standard iOS networking APIs.

title: Prevent template copy from following symlinks
description: copy_template_dir uses fs::copy on DirEntry paths without checking for symlinks; on most platforms this follows symlinks and can copy arbitrary files outside the template repo into the new project. This is a security risk if template repos are user-supplied. Use symlink_metadata to detect symlinks and either skip, copy as symlink, or enforce that resolved targets stay within the template repo. Affected: backend/crates/octo/src/api/handlers.rs::copy_template_dir.

title: 503 Service Unavailable on /code/ endpoints during agent reconnection
description:
Multiple requests to opencode proxy endpoints return 503 Service Unavailable:

- /code/session/{id}/message - fetching messages
- /code/config/providers - getting provider config  
- /code/session/{id}/prompt_async - sending prompts
- /code/command - running commands

Error in frontend:
'Failed to load live messages, falling back: Error: Request failed with 503'

This occurs during the windows when the opencode agent is disconnected/reconnecting. The backend correctly returns 503 when it cannot proxy to the opencode server, but the frequent agent cycling (see related bug) makes this happen repeatedly.

The frontend does have fallback logic ('falling back') but user experience is degraded during these periods.

title: Dictation overlay: live transcript not shown in main textarea
description:
In the Dictation overlay/modal, spoken text appears redundantly: the main textarea shows the composed message input, while interim speech appears in a separate small line below (liveTranscript).

Expected UX (confirmed): eliminate redundancy — dictated text should appear directly in the big top textarea in real time while speaking. The separate transcript line should not show duplicate content (can be removed or repurposed for a non-text status like "Listening…").

Actual: Main textarea only updates on final transcript segments; interim words render separately in the small line.

Notes: Current implementation intentionally keeps message input stable during dictation for performance; useDictation only calls onTranscript on final, while interim words go to liveTranscript.

Implementation direction: render textarea value as (committed value + liveTranscript) during dictation, and stop rendering liveTranscript as separate text.
close_reason: Fixed: Live transcript now appears directly in textarea during dictation

title: Add limits to zip download endpoints to prevent disk/CPU exhaustion
description: Zip creation for /download and /download-zip has no caps on total size, file count, or path count; a single request can create huge archives and fill disk/CPU. Add configurable limits (max total bytes, max entries, max depth) and fail early. Consider streaming without temp files or rejecting large directories. Affected: backend/crates/octo-files/src/handlers.rs (download, download_zip, create_zip_file_from_paths, create_zip_tempfile_blocking).
close_reason: Already fixed: ZipLimits enforced in enforce_zip_limits() -- max_bytes (500MB default), max_entries (10K default), configurable via config. Pre-scans before creating archive.

title: Release & Update System
description:
Comprehensive, bullet-proof release and update system for Oqto with transactional deployment semantics, strict preflight gates, staged rollout, and automatic rollback.

Goals
- Never leave host in half-updated state.
- Fail fast before deployment when prerequisites are missing.
- Guarantee mode-aware safety (single-user dev vs multi-user production).
- Provide deterministic rollback on failed health checks.
- Emit auditable update lifecycle events.

Scope
1) Preflight gate engine (hard-stop)
   - Validate target mode and required sandbox prerequisites.
   - Multi-user checks: /etc/oqto/sandbox.toml presence + owner/perms + readability.
   - If seccomp enforce configured: require /etc/oqto/seccomp/default.bpf.
   - Validate service dependencies, disk space, binary availability, and schema/config parse.

2) Transactional deployment layout
   - Versioned release directories + atomic symlink switch.
   - Prepare phase (build/upload/verify) separate from activate phase.
   - Auto-rollback to last known-good release on post-activate health failure.

3) Staged activation and health verification
   - Ordered restarts (runner/control-plane/dependent services).
   - Bounded readiness checks: runner socket, spawn smoke test, hstry/mmry connectivity.
   - Canary mode: update one host first, then fleet rollout.

4) Idempotent update CLI/API
   - Re-running update converges safely to desired state.
   - Resume support for interrupted updates with clear status reporting.

5) Observability and audit trail
   - Structured events: preflight.start/pass/fail, deploy.start/pass/fail, rollback.start/pass/fail.
   - Persist update metadata: release ID, host, actor, timestamps, decision reason codes.

Definition of done
- Multi-user host update fails before activation if sandbox prerequisites are missing.
- Successful update is atomic from operator perspective (no mixed-version runtime).
- Failed update auto-rolls back and restores previous healthy state.
- Update process is idempotent and documented with operator runbook.
- Canary + fleet rollout path is documented and tested.

title: Binary Release Pipeline
description: Build and distribute pre-compiled binaries for Linux (x86_64, arm64) and macOS (Intel, Apple Silicon). Eliminates need for Rust toolchain on user machines.

title: GitHub Actions workflow for cross-compilation
description: CI workflow using cross-rs or native runners to build for linux-x86_64, linux-arm64, darwin-x86_64, darwin-arm64

title: Release artifact packaging
description: Package binaries as tarballs with install script, checksums (SHA256), and signatures. Include octo, octoctl, fileserver binaries.

title: GitHub Releases integration
description: Automate publishing to GitHub Releases on tag push. Generate changelog from commits. Upload all platform artifacts.

title: Version embedding in binaries
description: Embed git tag/commit in binaries at build time. octo --version shows semver + commit hash.

title: Template Repository & Versioning
description: Separate templates into dedicated repo with version control. Enable template updates independent of binary releases. Track installed template versions per-agent.

title: Create octo-templates repository
description: New repo with versioned templates: AGENTS.md variants, opencode.json presets, plugins, scaffold templates. Semantic versioning independent of octo core.

title: Template manifest format
description: Define manifest.json schema: template metadata, version, compatibility range, variables/placeholders, dependencies between templates.

title: octoctl templates command
description: CLI for template management: list, install, update, diff. Track installed versions in ~/.config/octo/templates.lock

title: Agent template version tracking
description: Store template version used when creating agent. Enable 'octoctl agent update-templates' to upgrade individual agents.

title: Self-Update Command
description: octoctl self-update command that downloads latest binary, verifies checksum, replaces current binary, and restarts service. Support for update channels (stable/beta).

title: Update check endpoint
description: GitHub API or dedicated endpoint to check latest version. Compare with installed version. Cache results to avoid rate limits.

title: Binary download and verification
description: Download correct platform binary, verify SHA256 checksum, optionally verify GPG signature. Atomic replacement of current binary.

title: Service restart orchestration
description: Gracefully stop running sessions, replace binary, restart systemd/launchd service. Handle in-flight requests. Rollback on failure.

title: Update channels (stable/beta/nightly)
description: Support multiple release channels. Config option to set preferred channel. Beta gets release candidates, nightly gets every commit.

title: Proxmox LXC Runtime
description: New runtime backend using Proxmox API to provision LXC containers for agent sessions. Stronger isolation than Docker, native systemd support, persistent containers with snapshots.

title: Proxmox API client
description: Rust client for Proxmox REST API. Authentication (API tokens), node discovery, LXC CRUD operations, exec/console access.

title: LXC template for agent containers

title: RuntimeBackend trait implementation for Proxmox
description: Implement create/start/stop/exec/logs for LXC. Map agent sessions to VMID range. Handle networking (bridge, NAT, port forwarding).

title: LXC snapshot support
description: Create snapshots before risky operations. Rollback on failure. Scheduled snapshots for long-running agents.

title: Proxmox cluster support
description: Distribute agent containers across cluster nodes. Handle migration. Resource balancing.

title: GPU passthrough for LXC
description: Pass NVIDIA GPU to LXC containers for local LLM inference. Share GPU across multiple containers.

title: Storage configuration

title: Octo backend installation on Proxmox host
description: Install octo binary, configure for Proxmox runtime mode, create API token for LXC management, set up systemd service.

title: LXC template download and preparation

title: octo-setup CLI wizard
description:
Expand octo-setup from config hydrator into interactive CLI wizard. Two modes:

1. **octo-setup wizard** (CLI) -- Interactive terminal wizard using dialoguer/inquire:

   - User mode (single/multi)

   - LLM provider selection + API key input
   - Workspace directory
   - JWT secret generation (production)
   - Admin user creation (production)
   - Caddy/HTTPS setup (production)
   - Generates octo.install.toml and hydrates all config files

2. **octo-setup hydrate** -- Stays as-is for non-interactive/CI use

setup.sh becomes thinner: handles system package installation, tool installation (agntz, mmry, scrpr, sx, tmpltr, sldr, ignr), SearXNG setup, systemd services, Pi extension cloning -- then delegates config generation to octo-setup wizard.

title: octo-setup web wizard
description:
Web-based setup wizard served by octo itself for first-run configuration. Triggered when octo starts with no config or incomplete config.

Flow:
- Deployment mode selection
- LLM provider + API key configuration
- Workspace directory
- Admin user creation (production)
- HTTPS/domain setup (production)

Implementation:
- Served as a standalone page at /setup (no auth required on first run)
- Simple multi-step form, no agent involvement
- Writes octo.install.toml and calls octo-setup hydrate internally
- Redirects to login/onboarding after completion
- Disabled once initial setup is complete (returns 404)

The web wizard covers the same config options as the CLI wizard but is accessible from a browser for remote server setup.

title: setup.sh: EAVS mandatory LLM proxy + octo user home dir
description:
Completed in commit c9a3fe41:

EAVS as mandatory LLM proxy:
- install_eavs() installs binary + gnome-keyring deps for headless servers
- configure_eavs() walks through providers (anthropic, openai, google, openrouter, groq, mistral), detects env vars, prompts for API keys
- API keys stored in env file, referenced via env:VAR_NAME in EAVS config
- EAVS master key auto-generated, written to both EAVS env and octo config
- Old direct provider selection removed - everything goes through EAVS
- install_eavs_service() creates systemd service (user or system)

octo system user with proper home (/home/octo):
- ensure_octo_system_user() idempotent, called early so config can be written before service install
- XDG dirs created (~/.config, ~/.local/share, ~/.local/state)
- Service units export HOME, XDG_CONFIG_HOME, XDG_DATA_HOME, XDG_STATE_HOME
- Tools find config naturally without --config overrides
- eavs.service runs as octo user alongside octo.service (Before=/Wants=)
- SearXNG data at ~octo/.local/share/searxng/

Path layout (multi-user):
- /home/octo/.config/octo/ - runtime config (config.toml, env)
- /home/octo/.config/eavs/ - EAVS config + env with API keys
- /home/octo/.local/share/eavs/ - EAVS keys.db
- /home/octo/.local/share/searxng/ - SearXNG source + venv
- /etc/octo/ - sandbox policy only (sandbox.toml, skdlr-agent.toml, baseline config)
- /var/lib/octo/workspaces/ - shared workspace dirs

Also improved print_summary:
- Service status section at top with color-coded running/stopped
- EAVS in service status and core binaries
- LLM section shows configured providers from eavs config
- Next steps detect which services need starting
- SearXNG requirements.txt installed before editable install (fixes build)

title: WebSocket handler swapping causes message leaks
description:
Multiple Pi chat instances share the same WebSocket connection via wsCache, swapping message handlers without proper cleanup. This causes old handlers to continue receiving and processing messages meant for new sessions.

## Root Cause

In frontend/features/main-chat/hooks/usePiChatStreaming.ts:

```typescript
// Reuse global WebSocket if already open
if (wsCache.ws?.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN) {
    wsCache.ws.onmessage = handleWsMessage;  // Just swap handler!
    isOwnerRef.current = true;
    return;
}
```

The old handler continues receiving messages from the WebSocket message buffer before the new handler takes over.

## Reproduction Steps

1. Open Main Chat with Session A
2. Start a message stream that takes time
3. Quickly switch to Session B
4. Observe: Some messages from Session A's stream appear in Session B

## Impact

- Messages leak between sessions
- User sees messages in wrong chat context
- Temporary confusion before messages are replaced
close_reason: Fixed: Frontend now validates session_id on all WebSocket messages and ignores messages from wrong sessions

title: Enforce size limits in write_file

title: Opencode chats feel laggy (session navigation + text input)
description:
Session navigation and text input in opencode chats are laggy; needs substantial performance optimization for responsiveness/snappiness.

Observed console violations (dev mode):
- [Violation] keydown handler took ~636ms
- [Violation] input handler frequently ~180-245ms
- [Violation] message handler took ~210-283ms

Build notes (bun run build):
- Build succeeded.
- Vite warning: frontend/lib/ws-client.ts is dynamically imported by frontend/hooks/use-a2ui.ts but also statically imported by frontend/components/app-context.tsx and frontend/hooks/use-ws-session.ts; dynamic import will not move module into another chunk.
- Large chunks after minification: dist/assets/index-BkCrzz-C.js ~1.97MB (gzip ~599KB), TerminalView ~645KB (gzip ~188KB), CanvasView ~329KB (gzip ~100KB).

Expected: Typing and switching sessions should stay responsive (under 16ms per frame ideally).
Actual: Key/input handlers are blocking hundreds of ms.

Needs clarification: whether this reproduces in production preview, what size the session list/chat history is, and whether lag correlates with websocket message traffic/streaming.

title: Data isolation bug: user can see other users' data if Linux user creation fails
description:
## Bug Description

In multi-user mode, if Linux user creation fails during user registration, the user was still created in the database. When they start a session, if the configuration is wrong or linux_users is disabled, their session could run as the backend user - potentially exposing other users' chat history and data.

## Root Cause

1. User registration (auth.rs, admin.rs) caught Linux user creation errors but only logged warnings, allowing the DB user to persist
2. Session start (runtime.rs) fell back to `RunAsUser::current()` if `linux_users.enabled` was false

## Fix Applied

### 1. Roll back DB user if Linux user creation fails (auth.rs, admin.rs)
- Create DB user first (generates the user ID)
- Attempt to create Linux user
- If Linux user creation fails, DELETE the DB user and restore invite code
- Return error to the client - registration fails cleanly

### 2. Defense-in-depth at session start (runtime.rs)
- If `single_user=false` but `linux_users.enabled=false`, refuse to start session
- This catches misconfiguration that would cause data leakage

## Why not create Linux user first?

The user ID is generated from the username with collision detection, which requires DB access. Creating Linux user first would require either:
- Pre-generating IDs without collision check (risky)
- Separate ID generation query (more complexity)

The rollback approach is simpler and the window for inconsistency is tiny (between DB insert and Linux user creation).

## Files Modified

- `backend/crates/octo/src/api/handlers/auth.rs` - Delete DB user if Linux user fails
- `backend/crates/octo/src/api/handlers/admin.rs` - Delete DB user if Linux user fails
- `backend/crates/octo/src/local/runtime.rs` - Block sessions with unsafe configuration
close_reason: Fixed: user creation now fails if Linux user cannot be created, and sessions refuse to start with unsafe multi-user configuration

title: Add server-side browser feature (Option B) using agent-browser
description:
Server-side browser for AI agent control, rendered in Octo frontend.

## Reference Implementations (cloned)
- ../external-repos/agent-browser - Vercel's CLI browser automation (Apache-2.0)
- ../external-repos/playwriter - MCP browser extension by remorses (Option A reference)
- ../external-repos/rrweb - DOM mirroring library for efficient streaming
- ../external-repos/clawdbot/src/browser - Mature CDP+Playwright hybrid

## Recommended Stack
- **Option B (Server Browser):** Use vercel-labs/agent-browser
  - Built-in WebSocket screencast streaming (AGENT_BROWSER_STREAM_PORT)
  - CLI-first design, works well with MCP tools
  - Ref-based selectors from accessibility snapshots
  - Session isolation per user
  - Apache-2.0 license
  
- **Option A (Extension Mode):** Fork remorses/playwriter (see octo-3trr)
  - Chrome extension connects to user's existing browser
  - User's logins/extensions work
  - Single 'execute' tool with full Playwright API

## Rendering Approach
- Primary: agent-browser's built-in screencast (CDP Page.screencastFrame)
- Future: rrweb DOM mirroring for lower bandwidth (see ../external-repos/rrweb)

## Credential Management
- Tier 1: Fresh context (no auth)
- Tier 2: User profile (opt-in per session)  
- Tier 3: Credential vault with human-in-the-loop approval

## Key Decisions
- Use agent-browser's BrowserManager API for server browser
- Stream via WebSocket to BrowserView component
- Forward mouse/keyboard events back to server
- MCP tools shell out to agent-browser CLI

## Related
- octo-3trr: Browser extension mode (Option A)
- octo-58xa: Agent WebView for custom web apps
close_reason: Implemented: agent browser integrated in backend, UI, and setup

Install agent-browser as dependency. Create BrowserService in backend that:

title: Human-in-the-loop: Credential access approval modal

Browser: User interaction handoff mode (OAuth, captcha, 2FA)
description:
When agent encounters OAuth, captcha, or 2FA, it needs to hand control to user.

## Flow
1. Agent detects auth page or blocker
2. Agent calls: browser_request_user_action({ reason: 'Please log in to GitHub' })
3. Frontend shows 'USER ACTION REQUIRED' banner
4. BrowserView becomes fully interactive (user can click/type)
5. User completes OAuth/captcha/2FA
6. User clicks 'Done - Resume Agent'
7. Agent resumes control, verifies success via snapshot

## Implementation

### New MCP Tool
browser_request_user_action:
  - reason: string (shown to user)
  - blocking: bool (agent waits for completion)
  - timeout_ms: optional

### Frontend State
type BrowserMode = 'agent_control' | 'user_action'

### Protocol
Agent → Frontend: { type: 'request_user_action', reason, requestId }
Frontend → Agent: { type: 'user_action_complete', requestId }

## References
- ../external-repos/playwriter - Has collaborative mode concept
- ../external-repos/clawdbot/src/browser - Extension attach/detach pattern

title: Upgrade OpenCode to 1.1.10+ to address CVE-2026-22813 (XSS/RCE vulnerability)
description:
## Summary

Critical XSS vulnerability in OpenCode web UI (CVE-2026-22813) allows malicious websites to execute arbitrary commands via /pty/ endpoints.

**Advisory:** https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/security/advisories/GHSA-c83v-7274-4vgp

## Impact on Octo

- **Container mode**: Lower risk due to network isolation, but containers should be rebuilt
- **Local mode**: Higher risk - OpenCode runs natively and could expose localhost:4096

## Required Actions

1. Pin OpenCode version to >=1.1.10 in container/Dockerfile
2. Update frontend/Dockerfile npm install to use specific version
3. Document upgrade path for local mode users
4. Rebuild and publish new container images

## Related

- See also: octo-af5j.7.3 (bundle exact opencode versions)

title: Test issue
Sandbox Security Enhancements: Custom Profiles, FUSE Guard, SSH Proxy
description:
Epic for enhancing octo-sandbox with custom profiles, runtime access control via FUSE, and SSH agent proxying.

## Overview

Three major enhancements to the sandbox security model:

1. **Custom Profiles** - Allow users to define custom sandbox profiles in sandbox.toml
2. **FUSE Guard (octo-guard)** - Runtime file access control with user prompts and audit logging
3. **SSH Agent Proxy (octo-ssh-proxy)** - Controlled SSH access without exposing private keys

## Architecture

```
bwrap/sandbox-exec (hard isolation)
├── deny_read: ~/.ssh, ~/.gnupg, ~/.aws (blocked)
├── allow_write: ~/.cargo, /tmp (direct)
├── /guarded (FUSE) ──► octo-guard (runtime approval)
└── $SSH_AUTH_SOCK ──► octo-ssh-proxy ──► real ssh-agent
```

## Components

### 1. Custom Profiles (DONE - needs testing)
- [x] SandboxProfile struct
- [x] SandboxConfigFile with [profiles.*] sections
- [x] Profile resolution (custom > builtin > fallback)
- [x] Workspace can reference global profiles
- [ ] Update example sandbox.toml
- [ ] Documentation

### 2. FUSE Guard (octo-guard)
- Runtime access control for "gray area" paths
- User prompts for sensitive files
- Audit logging
- Linux only (macOS: fallback to socket+copy broker)

### 3. SSH Agent Proxy (octo-ssh-proxy)
- Proxy between sandbox and real ssh-agent
- Policy: allowed_hosts, allowed_keys
- Prompt for unknown hosts
- Audit logging
- Runs per-user, outside sandbox

## Security Model

| Layer | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| bwrap/sandbox-exec | Hard blocks, namespace isolation |
| octo-guard (FUSE) | Runtime approval, audit |
| octo-ssh-proxy | SSH access without key exposure |

## Cross-Platform

| Feature | Linux | macOS |
|---------|-------|-------|
| Custom profiles | ✓ | ✓ |
| FUSE guard | ✓ | ✗ (socket broker fallback) |
| SSH proxy | ✓ | ✓ |
close_reason: All P2 tasks complete: Custom profiles, FUSE guard (octo-guard), SSH proxy (octo-ssh-proxy), prompt system, and ttyd Unix socket security. P3 macOS fallback (octo-mxd8.4) remains open for future work.

Update the example sandbox.toml and documentation for the new custom profile feature.

## Tasks

- [ ] Document workspace profile inheritance

## Example Config

```toml
[profiles.my-custom]
deny_read = ["~/.ssh", "~/.gnupg", "~/.aws", "~/.kube"]
allow_write = ["~/.cargo", "/tmp"]

isolate_pid = true

[profiles.airgapped]

```

title: Implement octo-guard FUSE filesystem for runtime access control
description:
Implement a FUSE-based filesystem that provides runtime access control for "gray area" paths that need user approval.

## Overview
octo-guard runs outside the sandbox and provides a FUSE mount at /guarded. Paths configured in `guarded.paths` are symlinked into the sandbox pointing to the FUSE mount.

## Features
- [ ] Basic FUSE filesystem using `fuser` crate
- [ ] Policy engine (auto/prompt/deny per path)
- [ ] User prompt mechanism (CLI or UI callback)
- [ ] Audit logging
- [ ] Session-based approval caching
- [ ] Timeout/revocation support

## Config
```toml
[profiles.development.guarded]
enabled = true
paths = ["~/.kube", "~/.docker", "~/.config/gcloud"]

[profiles.development.guarded.policy]
"~/.kube/config" = "prompt"
"~/.docker/config.json" = "auto"
```

## Integration with bwrap
- Mount FUSE at /guarded
- Symlink guarded paths: ~/.kube → /guarded/home/user/.kube
- Hard-denied paths still blocked by bwrap (defense in depth)

## Platform
- Linux only (FUSE)
- macOS fallback: socket + copy broker (separate task)
close_reason: Implemented octo-guard FUSE filesystem: mounts virtual filesystem exposing guarded paths, enforces policy (auto/prompt/deny), integrates with prompt system for user approval, caches session approvals

title: Implement octo-ssh-proxy for controlled SSH agent access
description:
Implement an SSH agent proxy that allows sandboxed agents to use SSH without exposing private keys.

## Overview
octo-ssh-proxy runs per-user outside the sandbox, speaks SSH agent protocol, and forwards requests to the real ssh-agent after policy checks.

## Architecture
```
Sandbox                          User Space
┌─────────────────┐              ┌─────────────────┐
│ $SSH_AUTH_SOCK ──┼─────────────►│ octo-ssh-proxy  │
│ (bound socket)  │              │ (policy check)  │
└─────────────────┘              └────────┬────────┘
                                          │
                                          ▼
                                 ┌─────────────────┐
                                 │ real ssh-agent  │
                                 │ (holds keys)    │
                                 └─────────────────┘
```

## Features
- [ ] SSH agent protocol implementation (use ssh-agent-lib or similar)
- [ ] Policy: allowed_hosts (glob patterns)
- [ ] Policy: allowed_keys (filter by comment/fingerprint)
- [ ] Prompt for unknown hosts
- [ ] Audit logging (all sign requests)
- [ ] Lifecycle management (start/stop with sandbox)

## Config
```toml
[profiles.development.ssh_proxy]
enabled = true
socket = "/run/user/$UID/octo-ssh.sock"
allowed_hosts = ["github.com", "gitlab.com", "*.internal.corp"]
allowed_keys = ["work_ed25519"]  # empty = all
prompt_unknown_hosts = true
log_connections = true
```

## Lifecycle
- Local mode: octo server starts proxy on demand
- Runner mode: octo-runner starts one per user

## Platform
- Linux: ✓
- macOS: ✓ (same approach, different socket path)
close_reason: Implemented octo-ssh-proxy binary: SSH agent protocol proxy with policy enforcement (allowed_hosts, prompt_unknown), integrates with prompt system for user approval

title: macOS fallback: socket broker for guarded paths
description:
Implement a non-FUSE fallback for macOS that provides similar functionality to octo-guard using a socket-based broker.

## Overview
Since FUSE on macOS is problematic (kext deprecation, SIP issues), implement a simpler socket+copy approach.

## Architecture
```
Sandbox                          User Space
┌─────────────────┐              ┌─────────────────┐
│ /run/octo.sock ─┼─────────────►│ octo-broker     │
│ /tmp/granted/   │◄─────────────┤ (copies files)  │
└─────────────────┘              └─────────────────┘
```

## Workflow
1. Agent calls `octo-access request ~/.kube/config`
2. Request goes to octo-broker via socket
3. Broker checks policy, prompts user if needed
4. If approved, copies file to /tmp/granted/
5. Agent reads from /tmp/granted/

## Limitations vs FUSE
- File is copied, not live access
- Changes don't sync back
- Requires explicit request (not transparent)

## Features
- [ ] octo-broker daemon
- [ ] octo-access CLI (for use inside sandbox)
- [ ] Same policy config as octo-guard
- [ ] Audit logging

## Platform
- macOS: Primary target
- Linux: Can also be used as simpler alternative to FUSE
close_reason: Oqto targets Linux only; macOS sandbox fallback not needed

title: Network proxy for granular network access control
description:
Implement a network proxy that allows granular control over network access from sandboxed agents.

## Overview
Currently network isolation is binary (on/off). A proxy allows whitelisting specific domains while blocking everything else.

## Architecture
```
Sandbox (isolate_network=true)      User Space
┌─────────────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐
│ HTTP_PROXY=localhost:X ─┼────────►│ octo-net-proxy  │
│ HTTPS_PROXY=localhost:X │         │ (domain filter) │
└─────────────────────────┘         └────────┬────────┘
                                             │
                                    ┌────────▼────────┐
                                    │ Internet        │
                                    │ (filtered)      │
                                    └─────────────────┘
```

## Features
- [ ] HTTP/HTTPS proxy (e.g., using hyper)
- [ ] Domain allowlist
- [ ] Request logging/audit
- [ ] Bandwidth limits (optional)
- [ ] Cache for package managers (optional)

## Config
```toml
[profiles.development.network]
mode = "proxy"  # "open" | "isolated" | "proxy"

[profiles.development.network.proxy]
listen = "127.0.0.1:18080"
allow_domains = [
    "crates.io", "static.crates.io",
    "registry.npmjs.org",
    "github.com", "api.github.com",
]
log_requests = true
```

## Notes
- Proxy approach works cross-platform
- Doesn't require special permissions
- Some tools don't respect HTTP_PROXY (git, curl with certain flags)
close_reason: Network proxy functionality is handled by eavs (../eavs), not a separate component

title: Implement prompt system for security approvals
description:
Implement the prompt delivery system for octo-guard and octo-ssh-proxy to request user approval.

## Overview
When octo-guard or octo-ssh-proxy needs user approval, they send a prompt request to octo server, which routes it to the connected UI.

## Architecture
```
octo-guard/octo-ssh-proxy
    │
    ▼
POST /internal/prompt ──► octo server
                              │
                              ├─► WebSocket /api/prompts/ws ──► Octo UI (dialog)
                              │
                              └─► Desktop notification (fallback)
```

## Features

### Backend (octo server)
- [ ] Prompt queue management
- [ ] WebSocket endpoint for real-time prompts
- [ ] REST fallback endpoints
- [ ] Desktop notification integration (notify-rust)
- [ ] Timeout handling
- [ ] Audit logging

### Frontend (Octo UI)
- [ ] usePrompts WebSocket hook
- [ ] PromptDialog component
- [ ] Approval options: Allow Once, Allow Session, Deny
- [ ] Notification badge for pending prompts

### API
```
# WebSocket for real-time prompts
GET /api/prompts/ws

# REST endpoints
GET  /api/prompts           # List pending prompts
POST /api/prompts/:id       # Respond to prompt
     { "action": "allow_once" | "allow_session" | "deny" }

# Internal endpoint for guard/ssh-proxy
POST /internal/prompt
     { "source": "octo-guard", "type": "file_access", "path": "~/.kube/config", ... }
```

## Config
```toml
[profiles.development.prompts]
desktop_notifications = true
auto_deny_timeout_secs = 30
```
close_reason: Implemented prompt system: backend (PromptManager, models, routes), frontend (usePrompts hook, PromptDialog components)

title: Codebase Refactoring for Maintainability
description:
Comprehensive refactoring initiative to improve code organization, reduce duplication, and establish clear separation of concerns across frontend and backend.

## Goals
- Break up large monolithic files (2000-5000+ lines) into focused modules
- Establish feature-based organization for frontend
- Standardize domain patterns in backend
- Reduce code duplication via shared utilities and type generation
- Improve testability through better separation of concerns

## Key Metrics
- No file exceeds 500 lines (target)
- Each module has single responsibility
- Types shared between frontend/backend via code generation
- Test coverage improved for refactored modules

## Phases
1. Quick Wins (1-2 days each) - High impact, low effort splits
2. Medium Effort (3-5 days each) - Hook decomposition, proxy factory
3. Foundation Work (1-2 weeks) - Type generation, comprehensive testing
close_reason:
Epic complete - all 13 tasks closed. Major refactoring achieved:
- Backend: handlers split (12 modules), proxy factory (6 modules), history domain standardized, type generation (25 types)
- Frontend: API client split (17 modules), AppShellRoute extracted (75% reduction), usePiChat decomposed (83% reduction), session-context split (4 contexts), feature-based organization (5 features), dashboard refactored, component library organized (4 categories)
- All builds pass, 387 backend tests + 45 frontend tests passing

title: Add comprehensive test infrastructure
description:
## Current State
Limited test coverage, tests scattered across the codebase without clear organization.

## Backend Test Structure
```
backend/crates/octo/
├── src/
│   └── */mod.rs           # Unit tests inline (#[cfg(test)])
└── tests/
    ├── common/
    │   ├── mod.rs         # Test utilities
    │   ├── fixtures.rs    # Test data factories
    │   └── mocks.rs       # Mock implementations
    ├── integration/
    │   ├── mod.rs
    │   ├── session_test.rs
    │   ├── auth_test.rs
    │   ├── api_test.rs
    │   └── main_chat_test.rs
    └── e2e/
        └── workflow_test.rs
```

## Frontend Test Structure
```
frontend/
├── __tests__/
│   ├── setup.ts           # Test setup, mocks
│   ├── utils/
│   │   ├── render.tsx     # Custom render with providers
│   │   └── mocks.ts       # API mocks
│   ├── hooks/
│   │   ├── usePiChat.test.ts
│   │   ├── useVoiceMode.test.ts
│   │   └── useWsSession.test.ts
│   ├── components/
│   │   └── *.test.tsx
│   └── integration/
│       └── *.test.tsx
└── vitest.config.ts
```

## Test Utilities to Create

title: Implement generic proxy factory in backend
description:
## Current State
`backend/crates/octo/src/api/proxy.rs` is 2,070 lines with repetitive proxy functions:
- proxy_opencode
- proxy_fileserver  
- proxy_terminal_ws
- proxy_voice_stt_ws
- proxy_voice_tts_ws
- proxy_sldr

All follow similar patterns with copy-pasted error handling and session lookup.

## Target Design

```rust
// proxy/builder.rs
pub struct ProxyBuilder {
    session_field: SessionField,
    path_prefix: Option<&'static str>,
    timeout: Duration,
    transform_request: Option<Box<dyn Fn(Request) -> Request>>,
}

pub enum SessionField {
    OpencodePort,
    FileserverPort,
    TtydPort,
    VoiceSttPort,
    VoiceTtsPort,
    Custom(Box<dyn Fn(&Session) -> u16>),
}

impl ProxyBuilder {
    pub fn http(field: SessionField) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn websocket(field: SessionField) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn with_timeout(self, timeout: Duration) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn with_path_prefix(self, prefix: &'static str) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn build_handler(self) -> impl Handler { ... }
}
```

## Usage Example
```rust
// routes.rs
.route("/session/{id}/code/{*path}", 
    ProxyBuilder::http(SessionField::OpencodePort)
        .with_timeout(Duration::from_secs(300))
        .build_handler()
)
.route("/session/{id}/terminal",
    ProxyBuilder::websocket(SessionField::TtydPort)
        .build_handler()
)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] ProxyBuilder supports HTTP and WebSocket proxies
- [ ] Session field enum covers all current proxy targets
- [ ] Existing proxy behavior unchanged
- [ ] proxy.rs reduced to ~500 lines
- [ ] Error handling centralized in builder
- [ ] Timeout and transform hooks configurable

## Effort: Medium (3-4 days)
close_reason: Split proxy.rs into modular structure: builder.rs (507), handlers.rs (352), websocket.rs (143), terminal.rs (405), sse.rs (232), mmry.rs (478). Extracted common session lookup, validation, and HTTP proxy patterns.

title: Implement TypeScript type generation from Rust
description:
## Problem
Types are duplicated between:
- Rust structs (`session/models.rs`, etc.)
- TypeScript types (`control-plane-client.ts`, `types.ts`)

Changes require manual sync and types can drift apart.

## Solution
Use `ts-rs` crate to auto-generate TypeScript types from Rust structs.

## Implementation

### 1. Add ts-rs to Cargo.toml
```toml
[dependencies]
ts-rs = { version = "10", features = ["serde-compat"] }
```

### 2. Annotate Shared Models
```rust
// session/models.rs
use ts_rs::TS;

#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, TS)]
#[ts(export, export_to = "../../../frontend/generated/")]
pub struct Session {
    pub id: String,
    pub status: SessionStatus,
    pub workspace_path: String,
    // ...
}

#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, TS)]
#[ts(export, export_to = "../../../frontend/generated/")]
pub enum SessionStatus {
    Pending,
    Starting,
    Running,
    Stopping,
    Stopped,
    Failed,
}
```

### 3. Build Script / Just Command
```bash
# justfile
gen-types:
    cargo test export_bindings --features ts-rs
    # or use build.rs to auto-generate
```

### 4. Frontend Import
```typescript
// Use generated types
import type { Session, SessionStatus } from '@/generated/Session';
```

## Models to Generate
- Session, SessionStatus
- WorkspaceSession
- ChatSession, ChatMessage
- UserInfo
- Persona
- ProjectEntry, ProjectTemplate
- Settings types
- WebSocket event types

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] ts-rs configured in backend
- [ ] Key API types annotated with #[ts(export)]
- [ ] `just gen-types` generates frontend/generated/*.ts
- [ ] Frontend uses generated types
- [ ] CI validates types are in sync
- [ ] Manual types removed from control-plane-client.ts

## Effort: High (1 week)
close_reason: Added ts-rs for TypeScript generation. Annotated 25 types (Session, UserInfo, ChatSession, AgentInfo, etc.). Generated types in frontend/src/generated/. Added 'just gen-types' command.

description: Remove ~28 dead code items: PiSessionManager, PiSession, PiManagerConfig, PiSessionConfig, PiSessionCommand, PiEventWrapper, PendingResponses, PendingClientId, HstryExternalId, ModelCacheEntry, JsonlMessageKey, JsonlEntry, JsonlSessionInfoEntry, JsonlMessageEntry, StatsDelta, and all associated functions (message_signature, build_jsonl_key, build_metadata_json, compute_stats_delta, read_jsonl_message_entries, read_jsonl_session_name, resolve_jsonl_message_indices, fetch_last_hstry_idx, resolve_jsonl_session_title). title: Fix ts-rs serde attribute parse warnings in canon/types.rs
description:
164 'failed to parse serde attribute' warnings from ts-rs when processing canon/types.rs. These are generated by ts-rs v10 when it encounters serde attributes it doesnt understand (like skip_serializing_if, is_default_format custom functions etc).

Options:
1. Update ts-rs to a version that handles these attributes (check if v11+ fixes this)
2. Add ts(skip) or ts-specific attributes alongside serde ones where needed
3. If neither works, this may require restructuring the serde attributes or filing upstream

These warnings are noisy but cosmetic - they dont affect correctness.

title: Replace unsafe libc calls with nix crate safe wrappers
description:
Replace all unsafe libc calls with safe alternatives from the nix crate (already listed in workspace Cargo.toml):

1. api/handlers/projects.rs - libc::geteuid()/getegid() (4 blocks) -> nix::unistd::geteuid()/getegid()
2. local/sandbox.rs - libc::getpwnam + pointer deref (2 blocks) -> nix::unistd::User::from_name()
3. runner/client.rs - libc::getpwnam + pointer deref (2 blocks) -> nix::unistd::User::from_name()
4. agent_browser.rs - libc::kill for process signaling (1 block) -> nix::sys::signal::kill(Pid, Signal)

title: Replace unsafe termios code in octoctl with rpassword crate
description: ctl/main.rs uses 4 unsafe blocks (lines 2274-2291) for terminal echo suppression during password input via raw libc::tcgetattr/tcsetattr. Replace entire read_password() function with the rpassword crate which provides a safe, cross-platform rpassword::read_password() function that handles all the terminal manipulation safely.

title: Replace unsafe env::set_var in octo-runner and test code
description:
Two categories of unsafe env::set_var/remove_var:

1. bin/octo-runner.rs line 3650 - set_var at startup for .env loading. Replace with a proper env file loading approach: either collect all vars into a HashMap and apply them before spawning threads, or use a crate like dotenvy which handles this safely.

2. auth/config.rs tests (4 blocks) and local/sandbox.rs tests (3 blocks) - set_var/remove_var in tests. Replace with the temp_env crate (temp_env::with_var/with_vars) which provides scoped env var overrides that are automatically cleaned up, or use serial_test with proper isolation.

title: Unable to switch models in chat session
description: Model picker/switcher in the chat UI does not work — user cannot change the active model for a session. May be related to get_models/set_model RPC commands not being sent or handled correctly, or the UI not reflecting available models from the Pi harness.
close_reason: Fixed: pi_manager.set_model/cycle_model/set_thinking_level/cycle_thinking_level now wait for Pi's response via pending_responses (instead of fire-and-forget send_command). Runner returns PiModelChanged/PiThinkingLevelChanged instead of PiCommandAck. ws_multiplexed also emits ConfigModelChanged event on successful set_model/cycle_model.

title: test
Messages saved with stale pi_session_id when switching sessions
description:
When users switch between Main Chat sessions, the WebSocket handler captures pi_session_id once at connection time and never refreshes it. This causes messages to be persisted to the database with the wrong session_id.

## Root Cause

In backend/crates/octo/src/api/main_chat_pi.rs, the ws_handler captures:

```rust
// Snapshot Pi session id for traceability (best-effort).
let pi_session_id = session.get_state().await.ok().and_then(|s| s.session_id);
```

This pi_session_id is then used when saving messages:

```rust
main_chat_svc.add_message(
    &user_id,
    CreateChatMessage {
        role: MessageRole::User,
        content,
        pi_session_id: pi_session_id.clone(),  // Stale!
    },
).await?;
```

## Reproduction Steps

1. Open Main Chat with Session A active
2. Switch to Session B (empty)
3. Send a message in Session B
4. Observe: Message appears in Session B but is saved with Session A's ID
5. When viewing Session A later, the message also appears there

## Impact

- Messages appear in wrong sessions
- User confusion and data integrity issues
- Chat history becomes corrupted
close_reason: Fixed: Backend now gets current session_id dynamically when saving messages instead of using stale snapshot

title: Security audit sudoers configuration for multi-user mode
description:
## Background

The sudoers configuration in setup.sh had critical security vulnerabilities that could allow privilege escalation. These have been fixed.

### Vulnerabilities Found and Fixed

1. **OCTO_USERADD - UID 0 injection** (CRITICAL)
   - Pattern `[0-9]*` allowed creating users with UID 0 (root equivalent)
   - Fixed: Regex restricts to `[2-9][0-9][0-9][0-9]` (2000-9999 range)

2. **OCTO_CHOWN_WORKSPACE - Arbitrary home takeover** (CRITICAL)
   - Pattern `/home/*` allowed chowning ANY user's home directory
   - Fixed: Regex restricts to `/home/octo_[a-z0-9_]+` only

3. **OCTO_START_USER - Arbitrary user systemd** (HIGH)
   - Pattern `user@*.service` allowed starting any user's systemd instance
   - Fixed: Regex restricts to `user@[2-9][0-9][0-9][0-9].service`

4. **Wildcard path traversal** (HIGH)
   - Sudoers wildcards in arguments match `/`, enabling `../../` traversal
   - Fixed: Using regex patterns which don't match unintended characters

5. **Wrong user for production** (MEDIUM)
   - Sudoers used `$(whoami)` which is the admin, not the service user
   - Fixed: In multi-user mode, sudoers are created for `octo` system user

## Production Setup Flow

1. Admin runs `./setup.sh` with full sudo access
2. Setup installs dependencies, builds binaries to `/usr/local/bin/`
3. Creates `octo` system user (nologin shell)
4. Creates sudoers rules for `octo` user (not admin)
5. Installs systemd service running as `User=octo`
6. `octo` user can ONLY run the restricted sudo commands

## Security Audit

Run `./scripts/test-sudoers-security.sh` to verify regex patterns.

For runtime testing, need to test as the `octo` user (not an admin with full sudo).

## Files Modified

- `setup.sh` - Secure regex patterns, uses `octo` user for production
- `scripts/test-sudoers-security.sh` - Pattern-based security tests
- `docs/security/SUDOERS_AUDIT.md` - Audit guide

title: Define context model and context sources (local + remote)
description:
## Goal
Define what "context" means for agent interactions in Octo, and how it is represented, versioned, and sourced, so features like global agent invoke (`octo-skks`) and agent-driven UI control (`octo-wzvn`) can reliably inject context now and later.

## Context Model (Proposed)
A versioned envelope composed of multiple context "sources".

### Envelope
- `version`: schema version (e.g. `1`)
- `timestamp`: when the context snapshot was generated
- `request_id`: correlation ID for tracing/logging
- `sources`: array of `{ type, id, trust, data }`

### Source Types (initial)
- `ui.route`: pathname, query params, active app/view
- `ui.selection`: selected workspace/project, selected session IDs, selected agent/persona IDs
- `workspace.local`: directory path, repo metadata (optional), open files/selection (optional)
- `runtime.sessions`: opencode base URL, running session state, capabilities

### Future Source Types
- `workspace.remote`: remote machine ID, remote CWD, remote repo metadata
- `runtime.remote`: remote agent/session IDs, remote tool capabilities
- `device`: OS/app version, connectivity state (optional)

## Remote Machines (Forward Requirements)
- Remote contexts must be identifiable (`machine_id`) and optionally scoped (`project_id`, `workspace_id`).
- Remote context acquisition must handle offline/partial data and include a `trust` level.
- Avoid leaking secrets: context injection should be redacted and size-limited.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A documented schema (fields + examples) that is stable and versioned.
- Clear rules for when context is snapshotted (on open, on send, per message) and how staleness is handled.
- Clear rules for redaction, size limits, and trust levels.
- Identifies integration points in frontend + backend message pipeline.

## Notes
Prefer structured JSON context injected as a single block (or a dedicated API field/header) rather than freeform text. Keep it deterministic for caching and debugging.

title: Global main agent invoke with context injection
description:
## Problem
Users want to invoke the Main Agent from any page in the web app, and have the agent automatically receive UI/runtime context (current page/route, active app/view, selected agent/persona if applicable, selected workspace directory/project, current session IDs).

## Proposed Feature
Add a globally-available Main Agent entrypoint (e.g., hotkey + floating button + command palette action) that opens the Main Chat/agent panel. When the user sends a message, inject a structured context block into the message/system prompt containing:
- Current route/pathname
- Active app/view (e.g. sessions/settings/admin)
- Active agent/persona (if any)
- Current workspace directory / project key
- Current workspace session ID + current chat session ID (if available)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Main Agent can be opened from any page without navigation side effects.
- Sent messages include the context injection reliably and deterministically.
- Context injection is visible in logs/devtools (or can be toggled) for debugging.
- Works when OpenCode is not running (falls back to disk/history context).
- No regression to existing Main Chat / Sessions flows.

## Notes
Implementation likely touches: app shell routing, global UI overlay, and the message send pipeline (control-plane / opencode proxy headers).

title: Multi-Runner & Workspace Sharing
description:
Epic for enabling users to connect to multiple runners across different machines (laptop, desktop, cloud) and share workspaces with other users.

## Goals

### Multi-Runner Support
- One user can register multiple runners (local laptop, desktop, cloud instance)
- Single WebSocket multiplexes sessions across all runners
- Runner endpoint types: Unix socket, network address, HTTPS URL

### Workspace Sharing
- Users can grant permissions to others for their workspaces
- Permission levels: Read, Write, Execute, Owner
- Backend proxy model: requests routed to owner's runner
- Full audit logging for cross-user access

## Use Cases

1. Alice has runners on laptop, desktop, and cloud - all accessible via single UI
2. Alice grants Bob read access to her project workspace
3. Bob views Alice's session and sends prompts (routed through Alice's runner)
4. Team shares development workspace with tiered permissions

## Architecture

Backend acts as permission gateway:
- User A accesses User B's workspace
- Backend checks permission in DB
- Backend forwards to User B's runner (runs as User B)
- Runner has no visibility of requesting user

## Security

- Runner runs as workspace owner, not requester
- All cross-user access logged
- TLS required for network runner endpoints
- Runner authentication to prevent spoofing

title: Security: Session services were binding to 0.0.0.0 instead of 127.0.0.1
description:
OpenCode, fileserver, and ttyd sessions spawned via octo-runner and local mode were binding to 0.0.0.0 (all interfaces) instead of 127.0.0.1 (localhost only). This exposed these services to the network, bypassing the octo backend proxy.

Fixed in:
- backend/crates/octo/src/bin/octo-runner.rs (lines 1227, 1248, 1277)
- backend/crates/octo/src/local/process.rs (lines 202, 259, 330)

Added 8 security tests to prevent regression:
- 4 tests in process.rs
- 4 tests in octo-runner.rs

Tests verify that --hostname/--bind/--interface args use 127.0.0.1 and explicitly check they are NOT 0.0.0.0.

Note: Container entrypoints intentionally still use 0.0.0.0 since containers need external access from the host for proxying.

title: Main Chat: Pi agent runtime integration
description:
Integrate pi (from pi-mono) as the agent runtime for Main Chat.

## Why Pi for Main Chat
- Block streaming (coherent chunks vs token-by-token)
- Built-in compaction with custom prompts
- Session tree with branching
- Evaluate if it's better than OpenCode

## Integration Approach

1. Spawn pi as subprocess (same as OpenCode currently)
2. Configure pi to use EAVS proxy for credentials
3. Pass configuration via:
   - Environment variables (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY = virtual key)
   - models.json pointing providers to EAVS baseUrl

## Credential Flow

Pi supports custom providers via models.json:
```json
{
  "providers": {
    "anthropic": { "baseUrl": "http://eavs:41800/v1" },
    "openai": { "baseUrl": "http://eavs:41800/v1" }
  }
}
```

EAVS handles actual credentials (API keys or OAuth tokens via eavs-hzhn).

## Tasks
- [ ] Install pi binary or build from source
- [ ] Create pi config generation (models.json for EAVS)
- [ ] Add spawn_pi to local/process.rs
- [ ] Create main chat pi workspace setup
- [ ] Figure out pi's HTTP/RPC API for frontend
- [ ] Test with EAVS virtual keys

## Depends On
- EAVS OAuth support (eavs-hzhn) for subscription auth

## Questions
- Does pi have an HTTP API like OpenCode? -> RPC mode on stdin/stdout
- How to handle session persistence? -> JSONL files
- Block streaming in frontend? -> Subscribe to events

Ref: OCTO_MAIN.md

Security: Runner authentication and TLS for network endpoints
description:
Implement runner authentication and TLS for network endpoints:

1. Runner authentication
   - Token-based auth when runner connects to backend
   - Runner identity verification
   - Prevent runner spoofing attacks
   - Auth token rotation

2. TLS for network runners
   - HTTPS support for SecureUrl endpoints
   - Certificate validation
   - Mutual TLS (mTLS) option for high-security
   - Configurable TLS settings

3. Security policies
   - Network runners must use TLS
   - Local Unix sockets allowed (trusted environment)
   - IP whitelist for network access
   - Rate limiting on registration

4. Key/certificate management
   - Generate certificates during runner setup
   - Certificate rotation schedule
   - Secure storage of credentials

5. Security audit
   - Review runner auth flow
   - Test for spoofing vulnerabilities
   - Validate permission isolation
   - Penetration testing checklist

Files:
- backend/crates/octo/src/runner/auth.rs (new)
- backend/crates/octo/src/runner/tls.rs (new)
- backend/crates/runner/src/config.rs (extend for TLS settings)

title: Performance: eliminate >50ms UI handlers
description:
Evidence
- Console violations: message handlers (>50ms), setTimeout handlers (>50ms), keydown handlers (~187ms), click handlers (>50ms), forced reflow (~43ms).
- React profiler capture: uploads/profiling-data.14-01-2026.18-45-18.json. Top total actual render time: AppShell (~7.7s, max 731ms), SessionsApp (~6.1s, max 365ms), AppProvider (~5.8s, max 732ms), MainChatEntry (~0.8s, max 7ms), DropdownMenu and StatusBar repeated small costs.

Causes and Fix Instructions
1) Main Chat WS message handler does per-token renders + clones and localStorage writes
- Where: frontend/hooks/usePiChat.ts (handleWsMessage, writeCachedSessionMessages, messages useEffect).
- Cause: each token appends to message + setMessages clones full array and parts; localStorage writes on every update; JSON.parse on each event.
- Fix: buffer token deltas in ref and batch UI updates via requestAnimationFrame or fixed interval (e.g., 50-100ms). Update message in place using immutable slice only for the streaming message. Write cache only on message completion or throttle to 1-2s. Consider moving JSON.parse + batching into a worker if needed.

2) Opencode session event handler triggers heavy refresh + logging on every event
- Where: frontend/features/sessions/SessionScreen.tsx (handleSessionEvent, requestMessageRefresh).
- Cause: console.log on most events; on message_start/end/tool events it invalidates cache + triggers loadMessages; repeated transitions cause large re-renders.
- Fix: gate logs behind debug flag; coalesce message refresh into a single scheduler (already partially throttled) but avoid refresh for high-frequency events; switch to incremental updates for deltas instead of re-fetching; only invalidate cache on message_end/tool_end; use a single transition per event batch.

3) Message merge is O(n log n) with nested scans
- Where: frontend/features/sessions/utils/mergeSessionMessages.ts.
- Cause: optimistic matching scans all next messages for each optimistic message; final map+sort runs on every refresh even when messages already sorted.
- Fix: pre-index next messages by fingerprint or by (role,text,timestamp) in a Map; avoid full sort by keeping stable order or detecting already-sorted; only merge changes for the last message when streaming.

4) Message grouping and rendering re-renders too much
- Where: frontend/features/sessions/SessionScreen.tsx (messageGroups, visibleGroups, MessageGroup component).
- Cause: groupMessages recomputed for full list on every message update; MessageGroup computes flatMap every render; no virtualization for long histories.
- Fix: incremental grouping (append-only updates) or memoize groups keyed by message id; memoize MessageGroup and derived values (allParts) with stable refs; introduce virtualization/windowing for message list; render only visible groups/rows.

5) Expensive derived computations on every message change
- Where: frontend/features/sessions/SessionScreen.tsx (tokenUsage useMemo, opencodeTodos useMemo).
- Cause: loops over all messages each update.
- Fix: maintain incremental counters in refs when new messages arrive; compute todos only on tool_end events or on demand; move expensive scans to background via useDeferredValue or worker.

6) Forced reflow from input resizing and scroll handlers
- Where: frontend/features/sessions/SessionScreen.tsx (setMessageInputWithResize, handleScroll, useLayoutEffect).
- Cause: writing style then reading scrollHeight; frequent scrollHeight reads + setState on scroll.
- Fix: use CSS auto-size (height: auto + max-height) with a ResizeObserver; if manual resize needed, read scrollHeight once per RAF and avoid writing then reading; throttle scroll handler to RAF and only set state when threshold actually changes.

7) Keydown/click jank from input handler and popup logic
- Where: frontend/features/sessions/SessionScreen.tsx (handleInputChange, handleInputKeyDown).
- Cause: per-keystroke startTransition + regex scans + multiple state updates, plus draft persistence timer.
- Fix: debounce popup state updates; move slash/mention parsing to a low-priority useDeferredValue; avoid unnecessary setState when popup state unchanged; coalesce draft writes to 1s.

8) Sidebar MainChatEntry refresh churn
- Where: frontend/features/main-chat/components/MainChatEntry.tsx.
- Cause: refreshSessionsUnconditional runs on sessionActivityTrigger; listMainChatPiSessions + localStorage write for every message.
- Fix: throttle refresh to fixed interval (e.g., 5-10s), skip if data unchanged; precompute search fields per session; avoid localStorage writes unless data changes.

9) AppShell/SessionsApp rerendering too high
- Where: frontend/routes/AppShellRoute.tsx, frontend/features/sessions/SessionsApp (via AppShell).
- Cause: top-level state updates (sessions, settings, search) force large tree to re-render.
- Fix: split AppShell into memoized subtrees; use context selectors to avoid propagating unrelated updates; move frequently changing state (message streaming) into isolated providers.

Verification Plan
- Capture fresh React Profiler + Chrome Performance recordings for main chat (Pi) and opencode sessions. Validate no violation messages for message/keydown/click/setTimeout; verify WS handlers < 16ms and no forced reflow warnings.
- Add targeted benchmarks around message merging and grouping (measure time per update in DEV with debug flag).

Notes
- If performance regressions persist, capture a new profile while streaming for at least 30s and update this epic with top offenders.
close_reason: Addressed major causes 1-8: buffered token updates via RAF, throttled localStorage writes, gated debug logging, optimized message merge with fingerprint indexing, throttled scroll handler, optimized popup state updates, throttled MainChatEntry refresh. Item 9 (AppShell subtree memoization) is lower priority.

title: Agent-driven UI control (conversational navigation)
description:
## Problem
Users want to navigate and operate the Octo web UI conversationally. The agent should be able to trigger UI actions (e.g., expanding/collapsing panels/canvas, switching apps/routes, opening dialogs, selecting sessions, focusing inputs) so the user can say what they want and the UI responds.

## Proposed Feature
Introduce a controlled "agent actions" layer that exposes safe UI commands to the agent (via tool calls / structured messages), such as:
- Expand/collapse canvas and sidebars
- Switch active app/view (sessions/settings/admin/etc.)
- Open/close dialogs (agent picker, settings, create session, etc.)
- Select a workspace/project, select a chat session
- Search and navigate to results
- Scroll/focus specific UI regions

## Safety / Guardrails
- Allowlist of actions only; no arbitrary DOM access.
- User confirmation for destructive actions (delete/stop session, reset settings).
- Clear UI indication when an action is triggered by the agent (audit trail / toast).
- Rate-limit or debounce repeated actions to prevent loops.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Agent can expand/collapse major layout regions (canvas/sidebar) via a structured action.
- Agent can navigate between main apps/routes and select sessions/projects.
- Destructive actions require explicit user confirmation.
- Actions are logged/traceable for debugging and support.

## Relationship
Complements `octo-skks` (context-aware global agent invoke) so the agent can both understand context and manipulate the UI intentionally.
close_reason: Implemented - UIControlProvider handles all ui.* WebSocket events
