Kept dead_code=allow for cross-binary false positives (proper fix is crate decomposition in oqto-gxf5). Zero warnings across workspace.

title: Audit and reduce useEffect usage in frontend
description:
The React team discourages useEffect for most use cases. We have 46 occurrences across 12 files, with AppShellRoute.tsx alone having 14.

Refactor candidates:
- Data fetching effects -> React Router loaders or TanStack Query
- Derived state effects -> compute during render
- Props-to-state sync -> use key prop to reset
- Event-driven logic -> move to event handlers

Legitimate uses to keep: WebSocket sync, subscriptions, one-time mount setup.

Priority files:
1. AppShellRoute.tsx (14 effects) - biggest win
2. hooks/useProjectActions.ts (6 effects)
3. SidebarSessions.tsx (5 effects)
4. hooks/useSidebarState.ts (4 effects)
5. hooks/useSharedWorkspaces.ts (3 effects)
6. Dialog components (2 each) - likely fine, but worth reviewing
close_reason: Completed comprehensive useEffect reduction and architecture refactor wave. AppShellRoute decomposed into focused hooks and now has 0 direct useEffect usage (from 13). Added reusable useMountEffect/useLocalStorage primitives, migrated sidebar/project localStorage state to hook-based primitives, moved dialog-open data loading in useProjectActions from effects into explicit handlers, and converted shared workspace initial load to mount hook. Added lint guardrail (scripts/check-useeffect-guardrail.mjs + baseline + package scripts) to prevent regressions. Frontend src useEffect count reduced from 34 to 19.

title: Instant chat history UX: cached-first rendering with hydration gating
description:
Hard requirement: opening any chat must render history instantly with no visible empty-state flash.

Status: Partially complete

Completed:

- Render cached messages immediately, then authoritative hstry sync in background
- Prevent 'No messages yet' from appearing before first authoritative fetch settles
- Fixed cache contamination: clear in-memory cache on session switch
- Fixed authoritative merge: only preserve isStreaming messages (no timestamp-based leaks)
- Frontend lint passes

Remaining:
- Session prewarm: prefetch latest messages for visible sessions (child task oqto-a0z4.1)

Success criteria:
- Opening an existing chat never flashes an empty timeline before history appears.
- If cache exists, content appears immediately (<1 frame) and later reconciles.
- If cache missing, show loading/skeleton until first authoritative response, then either messages or true empty state.
- Frontend lint passes.

title: Canonical timeline and hstry projection architecture
description: Design and implement Oqto's long-term canonical timeline architecture. Oqto-log/timeline becomes the authoritative, lossless, tree-aware conversation graph. hstry remains useful as cross-tool search/index/interoperability, but is demoted from primary Oqto timeline authority. This epic is guided by the Bitter Lesson: avoid narrow UI/harness-shaped DTOs; preserve rich raw data, stable identities, deterministic projections, context snapshots, and extensible schemas so future agents/tools/search/training systems can leverage accumulated data.
close_reason: Defined canonical timeline v1 protocol DTOs and documented oqto-log authority with hstry as rebuildable projection/search layer.

This replaces runner/UI-shaped ChatMessageProto as the long-term canonical data model while keeping serialized compatibility projections during migration.
close_reason: Implemented foundational timeline v1 architecture: DTOs, schema extensions/projection contract/docs/tests covering this slice.

Ensure final persisted snapshot is authoritative, IDs remain stable, abort/error/retry states are explicit, and replay produces identical timeline output.
close_reason: Added canonical event stream assembler that deterministically converts stream/tool/error events into timeline turns with raw envelopes and final snapshot precedence.

title: Define hstry as projection/search layer, not Oqto authority
description: Codify hstry's future role: cross-tool search/index/interoperability projection from oqto-log timeline, not primary Oqto timeline authority. Define flattened hstry projection documents with pointers back to oqto session_id, turn_id, message_id, branch_id, workspace_id, harness_session_id, and raw refs. Remove new primary Oqto reads/writes from hstry authority paths.
close_reason: Implemented foundational timeline v1 architecture: DTOs, schema extensions/projection contract/docs/tests covering this slice.

title: Build oqto-log to hstry projection pipeline
description: Implement async/idempotent projection from authoritative oqto-log timeline into hstry conversations/messages/search index. Include backfill, repair, reindex, and deletion/tombstone handling. hstry search results must deep-link back to exact Oqto timeline locations.
close_reason: Cancelled per ADR-0005: hstry fully decoupled from oqto. The oqto-log -> hstry projection pipeline is explicitly not built; hstry survives only as an optional standalone cross-agent search store.

Progress update (2026-05-08): Reviewed /design-exploration frontend tree mock (app.jsx/graph.jsx/sidepanel.jsx/data.js). It is a strong baseline for oqto-awh9.13 (graph canvas + transcript sidepanel + head/active-branch semantics + keyboard/filter UX). Next step: integrate against canonical timeline DTOs with feature flag and remove window-global mock wiring.

The preview adapts current DisplayMessage data into a typed linear tree/branch transcript, preserving the design-exploration split between graph and branch transcript. Remaining work: replace linear fallback with real TimelineView/TreeView API data and wire branch checkout/fork navigation.

TimelineTreeView uses real parent links when available and falls back to linear order only for legacy messages. This finishes the first production-safe frontend migration slice; future issues can replace compatibility message reads with native TimelineView API data and implement checkout/fork commands.

title: Wrapper and PATH interception strategy for secrets
Break up ctl/main.rs (~4,600 lines) into subcommand modules
Generic sandboxed-spawn RPC in oqto-runner/daemon/server.rs bypasses proxy egress
description: server.rs (~779) builds bwrap args + its own inline pre-exec (seccomp/no_new_privs/landlock) for a generic sandboxed spawn RPC, separate from pi_manager. It does NOT call SandboxConfig::prepare_egress, so a session spawned via this path under NetworkMode::Proxy would run with --unshare-net suppressed AND no egress namespace = fully open network (footgun). Before proxy mode is enabled in production: either wire egress here (prepare_egress + setns in its inline pre-exec via configure_bwrap_pre_exec + hold the guard for the process lifetime) or make this path fail-closed when network_mode==Proxy. Part of oqto-h6hr; primary pi_manager + oqto-sandbox CLI paths are already wired.

title: No context menu on shared workspace workdir folders - cannot rename or delete projects in shared workspaces. Workdir folder headers in SidebarSharedWorkspaces need ContextMenu wrapper with rename/delete/new chat actions.
Landlock silently skipped when disable_userns=true (affects all built-in profiles)
description:
## Problem

backend/crates/oqto-sandbox/src/spawn.rs:34 gates Landlock application on !config.disable_userns:

    let apply_landlock_pre_exec = !config.disable_userns;

All three built-in profiles (development, strict, minimal via customization) set disable_userns = true, so apply_landlock() is never actually called from pre_exec, despite landlock_mode = "audit" being the default in development and strict (config.rs:487, 548).

Net effect: the "Landlock support detected" debug log is printed while zero rules are installed. Users believe Landlock is active when it is not. This silently weakens the strict profile's security posture and makes audit-mode rollout meaningless.

## Why the gate exists

Applying Landlock in pre_exec before bwrap sets up user namespaces blocks the child's write to /proc/self/uid_map during --unshare-user setup, aborting the sandbox. So the current code chose "disable Landlock" over "break bwrap".

## Carmack fix

Apply Landlock AFTER bwrap completes namespace setup, not in pre_exec of the outer bwrap spawn. Options:

1. Wrap the inner command: bwrap ... -- oqto-sandbox-landlock-shim <real-cmd>, where the shim installs Landlock rules after entering the sandboxed namespace and then execve's the real binary.
2. Or: use bwrap's own mechanism if it grows Landlock support; until then option 1 is the portable path.

## Required changes

- Remove the !disable_userns gate from spawn.rs:34 and move Landlock application into the inner process (shim binary or equivalent).
- Ensure no_new_privs is still applied before Landlock (it is already, unchanged).
- Update tests under backend/crates/oqto-sandbox/tests/ to assert Landlock rules ARE active inside the sandbox even with disable_userns=true.
- Update docs/active/security/20260402-sandbox-profile-templates.md to reflect the fix.

## Verification

Negative test from outside:
    oqto-sandbox --config strict-profile.toml -- sh -c 'echo x > /etc/passwd'
must fail with EACCES in enforce mode. Positive test: workspace + allow_write paths remain writable.

## Scope

Only touches oqto-sandbox crate. No frontend/runner impact. Must land before we promote Landlock enforce in any production profile.
close_reason:
Fixed by moving Landlock application out of the outer bwrap pre_exec hook into an inner shim (crate::shim) that bwrap runs as its inner command after --unshare-user completes uid_map setup.

Changes:
- backend/crates/oqto-sandbox/src/shim.rs (new) — maybe_run_shim() + resolve_shim_binary() + run_shim_linux() (applies PR_SET_NO_NEW_PRIVS + Landlock then execvps the real command)

Verified:
- cargo test -p oqto-sandbox: 15/15 green
- cargo clippy -p oqto-sandbox --all-targets -D warnings: clean
- Dry-run with landlock_mode=enforce + disable_userns=true now emits --ro-bind for the shim and prefixes the inner command with /.oqto-sandbox-shim

Shim binary resolution: OQTO_SANDBOX_SHIM_BIN override > which(oqto-sandbox) on PATH > current_exe() if named oqto-sandbox. If not found, enforce mode aborts; audit mode warns and skips Landlock.

title: Fix identity-only cleanup querying unmigrated oqto-log DBs
description: Fixed identity sync cleanup opening discovered oqto-log sqlite files without migrating first. Added open_migrated_pool helper, made accidental migration repair no-op before _sqlx_migrations exists, and covered empty discovered DBs with a regression test.
close_reason: Fixed: identity cleanup migrates each discovered DB before querying via open_migrated_pool (oqto-history/oqto_log/ops.rs:55, used :428); regression test identity_cleanup_migrates_empty_discovered_databases. Verified.

title: Fix active get_messages path causing partial/disappearing assistant parts
Retry/error UX regression: spinner drops, empty assistant bubble, error lost on reload
description:
## Summary
Retry/error and disappearing-message regression work across runner/ws/frontend, with follow-up fixes for duplicate assistants and vanished tail messages.

## Additional finding from user report (kitt / sown-cubs-pint)
- Observed duplicate conversations sharing the same `platform_id` for the affected chat (`cmfy image generation`):
  - one populated conversation (message_count > 0)
  - one empty/stale alias conversation (message_count = 0)
- When resolution hit the empty exact alias first, the UI appeared to “lose” latest messages.

## Latest fix
### Backend identity resolution hardening
- `backend/crates/oqto/src/history/repository.rs`
  - `resolve_conversation_identity()` now computes `msg_count` during exact match lookup.
  - If exact match has `msg_count == 0` and has a `platform_id`, resolver now checks sibling conversations with same `platform_id` (same workspace when provided) and prefers a populated sibling (`msg_count > 0`).
  - Prevents transient empty-alias resolution from causing vanished timelines.

### Tests
- Added regression test:
  - `resolve_conversation_identity_prefers_populated_platform_sibling_for_empty_exact`
- Updated test fixture schema to include `messages` table used by resolver counts.

## Verification
- `cd backend && cargo test -p oqto resolve_conversation_identity -- --nocapture` ✅
- existing resolver tests still pass.

## Commits
- `47da754` — retry/error lifecycle + append-only ordering foundation
- `8e7573c` — duplicate assistant row fix in authoritative merge
- pending current delta: backend resolver hardening for empty alias conversations

## Current status
- **in_progress** until live confirmation in affected session path after backend restart.

title: P0 Epic: Deterministic live chat timeline integrity (streaming, session-switch, markdown)
description:
Structural hardening epic for remaining frontend timeline instability after oqto-log cutover.

Scope:
- Replace ad-hoc message mutations with a single reducer/state-machine for live + authoritative lanes.
- Enforce strict identity reconciliation (id/client_id only) and session alias equivalence across all event gates.
- Prevent message disappearance/reordering during stream, reconnect, partial sync, and rapid session switching.
- Guarantee first-turn optimistic user persistence and no stream loss on tab/session swaps.
- Eliminate raw markdown fallback regressions by deterministic DisplayPart render contract.
- Add regression suite for: streaming+switch loops, partial vs authoritative ordering, alias remap, markdown rendering stability.

Success criteria:
- No duplicated/disappearing messages in live sessions.
- No random text shifting/jumps after agent_end reconciliation.
- No raw markdown shown for normalized markdown parts.
- Repro sessions (e.g. Context Window Issues) stable under repeated switch stress.

Mandatory pre-commit verification checklist (must all pass before commit):
- [ ] Frontend unit tests for merge/reducer invariants pass (identity-only dedupe, ordering, alias equivalence).
- [ ] Targeted regression tests pass for:
  - [ ] streaming while switching sessions repeatedly,
  - [ ] partial vs authoritative merge ordering,
  - [ ] alias remap event acceptance,
  - [ ] optimistic first-turn persistence,
  - [ ] markdown part rendering stability (no raw markdown fallback).
- [ ] Manual E2E run with agent-browser on at least one known repro session and one fresh session:
  - [ ] no disappearing messages,
  - [ ] no duplicate user echo bubbles,
  - [ ] no random text shifting/jump after authoritative sync,
  - [ ] stream continues correctly after session switch away/back.
- [ ] Run frontend static checks:
  - [ ] `cd frontend && npx biome check features/chat/hooks/useChat.ts features/chat/hooks/message-utils.ts features/chat/components/ChatView.tsx components/data-display/markdown-renderer.tsx`
  - [ ] `cd frontend && bun run build`
- [ ] If backend touched, compile gate passes:
  - [ ] `cd backend && cargo check -p oqto`
- [ ] TRX hygiene before commit:
  - [ ] Epic/child statuses updated,
  - [ ] `.trx` synced via `trx sync`.
close_reason: Success criteria met: streaming timeline stable, no disappearing/duplicate messages, no raw markdown regressions

title: Pi-authoritative history pipeline: zero-loss external-session compatibility with canonical+hstry projection
description:
Goal
- Preserve instant history load/search while guaranteeing zero message loss and full fork/tree compatibility for Pi sessions, including sessions started/continued outside Oqto.

Architecture Decision
- For Pi sessions, Pi JSONL/native metadata is authoritative.
- hstry remains the fast canonical projection/index for frontend load + search.
- Frontend consumes canonical messages only.
- No heuristic multi-source merges in primary path.

Why
- Current failures come from mixed authority (Pi live/JSONL/hstry) and lossy legacy payload paths.
- External Pi sessions expose these mismatches first (duplicates, partial/disappearing assistant parts, fork/tree drift).

Scope
1) Authority + Sync Contract
- Define explicit identity mapping: pi_session_id <-> oqto_session_id <-> hstry_conversation_id (+ readable_id, parent/root lineage).
- Define staleness contract (fingerprint/cursor) for JSONL->hstry projection.

2) Deterministic Projection Engine
- Import/replay Pi JSONL to canonical message model losslessly (parts, tool calls/results, thinking, system/compaction markers).
- Idempotent upsert semantics (no duplicate writes on repeated persist cycles).
- Deterministic reindex command for repair (purge/rebuild projection from authoritative source).

3) Active Session Path Hardening
- Active workspace sessions must return canonical structured messages from runner authoritative path (buffer/hstry), never lossy legacy fallback.
- Streaming remains live via canonical events; final convergence to authoritative projection is guaranteed.

4) Fork/Tree Compatibility
- Reconstruct and persist parent/child/fork lineage from Pi-native data deterministically.
- Sessions started/continued outside Oqto appear with correct tree ancestry and complete messages.

5) hstry Size/SLOs
- Keep fast load/search; constrain growth (message_events retention/optional mode tracked in hstry issues).

Acceptance Criteria
- A Pi session started outside Oqto loads instantly in Oqto with complete message history and correct structured parts.
- Continuing that session outside Oqto and reopening in Oqto yields zero lost/duplicated messages.
- Forks created in Pi and Oqto produce identical tree ancestry in UI.
- No frontend merge heuristics required for correctness (id/client_id deterministic reconciliation only).
- Search and history load do not require starting a Pi process.
- End-to-end regression suite covers: external start/continue, compaction, tool-heavy streams, fork tree, reconnect.

Related
- oqto-3yqp (assistant responses disappear)
- oqto-9mtk (history appears partially lost)
- oqto-bcqj (active get_messages structured path)
- oqto-fmbr (duplicate persist idx bug)
- hstry: trx-jtxf, trx-aa3m (message_events retention/optional)
close_reason: All children complete: authority cutover, projection engine, runner buffer, fork lineage, repair, regression coverage

title: Runner: Pi process exits immediately after session.create in single-user mode (mobile chat hangs)
description:
## Summary
In local single-user mode, creating a new chat session succeeds, but the runner-managed Pi process exits almost immediately. The UI shows "working" briefly and then no assistant response arrives.

## Root Cause
User-installed Pi extensions in `~/.pi/agent/git/.../*/index.ts` (e.g. `pi-autoresearch`) import `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent`. Pi's system install at `/usr/local/lib/pi-coding-agent` had no node_modules entry for that name (the package IS the install, not a dependency), so bun's NODE_PATH-based resolution failed:

  Error: Failed to load extension ".../pi-autoresearch/index.ts": Cannot find module '@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent'

Pi exited 0 immediately after the error. Runner saw EOF -> "Pi[id] stdout reader finished (process exited)" -> next stdin write hit "Failed to write command: Failed to write to stdin".

The bun-global install at `~/.bun/bin/pi` (where `bun install -g` lands) was unaffected because module resolution naturally finds `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent` at its own install path. The runner picked the system install due to a Feb 2026 hardcode (commit 7be10d6) intended to prevent stale per-user installs from shadowing newer system ones.

## Fix
Two-layer change:

1. **Setup-level (root cause)**: `scripts/setup/05-install-core.sh` and `deploy/docker/Dockerfile` now create a self-link inside the system install:

       /usr/local/lib/pi-coding-agent/node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent
         -> /usr/local/lib/pi-coding-agent

   With NODE_PATH=$PI_PKG/node_modules (already set by the wrapper), extensions can now resolve `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent` against the system install. Both Pi installs become equivalent for correctness.

2. **Runner-level (simplification)**: `backend/crates/oqto/src/runner/daemon/config.rs::resolve_pi_binary` reverted to the simple model: honor an absolute `pi.executable`, otherwise `which` against PATH, with `/usr/local/bin/pi` and `/usr/bin/pi` as final fallbacks. The version-comparison heuristic (added as a workaround for the broken extension resolution) is gone.

## Live remediation
For deployments where setup ran before the fix, apply the link manually:

    sudo mkdir -p /usr/local/lib/pi-coding-agent/node_modules/@mariozechner
    sudo ln -sfn /usr/local/lib/pi-coding-agent \
      /usr/local/lib/pi-coding-agent/node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent

Then `just deploy` (rebuilds + restarts oqto-runner).

## Verification (post-fix)
Runner log shows:

    Pi binary: /usr/local/bin/pi (v0.70.5)

and a new chat session stays alive (no "process exited" errors).

## Update workflow (single-user dev)
- `bun install -g @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent@latest` updates ONLY `~/.bun/bin/pi`. The system install and self-link stay stale, but no longer break extensions.
- `just update-pi` is the canonical path: it bumps both halves and restarts the runner.

## Update workflow (multi-user / docker)
- `deploy/docker/Dockerfile` bakes Pi into the image with the self-link in place. `just deploy` rebuilds.
- For bare-metal multi-user hosts, admins run `just update-pi` on the host. All Linux users share `/usr/local/bin/pi`.

title: Integrate trx-core in oqto-runner; remove trx CLI shellout
description:
## Goal

Replace the per-request `Command::new("trx")` subprocess in oqto's TRX handlers with in-process trx-core calls running inside the per-user oqto-runner. Eliminates ~90ms subprocess+CRDT-load floor on every TrxView list/create/update/close, and removes the multi-user usermgr shell-out branch entirely.

## Architecture

Currently `backend/crates/oqto/src/api/handlers/trx.rs::exec_trx_command` either:
- spawns the trx CLI directly (single-user mode), or
- routes through usermgr -> trx CLI as the linux user (multi-user mode).

Both paths are subprocess spawns and bypass the runner. Per CLAUDE.md "actions go through runners" — the runner already runs as the right linux user with the right home/CWD, and is long-lived.

Target flow:
1. WS `trx` channel command -> oqto handler
2. Resolve runner via existing `resolve_runner_for_workspace_path`
3. `RunnerClient::trx_list/create/update/close` over the runner socket
4. Runner handler opens `trx_core::UnifiedStore::open_at(workspace_path)` (uses the new `open_at` API in trx 0.4.1) and performs the op in process
5. Both single-user and multi-user share this path; usermgr/CLI shellout for trx is deleted

## Scope

Backend (oqto crate):
- Add `trx-core = { git = "https://github.com/byteowlz/trx", branch = "master" }` to backend workspace deps
- runner/protocol.rs: add `TrxList/TrxCreate/TrxUpdate/TrxClose` request variants, `TrxIssueData` wire struct, `TrxList`/`TrxIssue` response variants
- runner/daemon/server/handlers/trx.rs: new dispatcher
- runner/daemon/server.rs: 4 Runner methods that call trx-core
- runner/daemon/server/handlers/dispatch.rs: route Trx* variants
- runner/client.rs: add `trx_list/trx_create/trx_update/trx_close` methods
- api/handlers/trx.rs: replace `exec_trx_command` with runner client calls; delete subprocess + usermgr branches; delete `Command::new` import; `From<TrxIssueRaw>` becomes `From<TrxIssueData>`
- Auto-init on `NotInitialized` preserved (handler creates `.trx` layout matching trx-core's `CrdtStore::init` and retries)

Out of scope:
- Frontend untouched (same WS channel)
- TS types regen unaffected
- `trx sync` endpoint stays as-is for now (can move to runner in a follow-up)

## Acceptance

- TrxView list/create/update/close all work via the runner with no `trx` subprocess in `ps`
- Per-call latency drops from ~90-200ms to <10ms in single-user mode
- `cargo clippy -p oqto` zero warnings
- `just lint-rust-ai-guardrails` passes
- Frontend lint+build clean
close_reason: Replaced exec_trx_command shell-out with in-process trx-core calls running inside oqto-runner. New runner protocol variants TrxList/TrxCreate/TrxUpdate/TrxClose; new RunnerClient methods; oqto WS handlers now go: validate_workspace_path -> resolve_runner -> client.trx_*. Auto-init on NotInitialized preserved by replicating CrdtStore::init layout in the runner. sync_trx kept shelling out (out of scope, low traffic). Verified: cargo build clean, cargo clippy -p oqto zero warnings, 327 lib tests pass, just lint-rust-ai-guardrails clean. Pre-existing test_default_config assertion fixed to accept either /usr/local/bin/pi or ~/.bun/bin/pi (was hardcoded to .bun path).

title: Security: Defense-in-depth sandboxing with seccomp-bpf fallback
description:
Current sandboxing relies solely on bwrap (bubblewrap). This is a single point of failure—if bwrap has vulnerabilities or is not available, agents run completely unsandboxed. Additionally, bwrap requires elevated privileges (setuid) which is a security risk itself.

## Current State

- Primary: bwrap with configurable profiles
- Fallback: None (runs unsandboxed)
- No seccomp-bpf or Landlock integration

## Requirements

1. seccomp-bpf Fallback
   - Compile BPF filters for common agent operations
   - Block dangerous syscalls (execve, ptrace, etc.)
   - Deny-by-default with allowlist approach
   - libseccomp or rust-seccomp integration

2. Landlock Support (Modern Linux)
   - Filesystem sandboxing without setuid
   - Composable with seccomp for defense-in-depth
   - Fallback to bwrap if Landlock unavailable

3. Capability Dropping
   - Drop all capabilities after sandbox setup
   - No-new-privs flag enforcement
   - User namespace support where available

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] seccomp-bpf filter blocks high-risk syscalls
- [ ] Graceful fallback chain: bwrap → landlock → seccomp → warning
- [ ] Capability audit shows minimal required set
- [ ] Sandbox escape test suite (attempt known techniques)
- [ ] Performance: <5% overhead on tool execution

## Related
- sandbox.toml configuration
- Current sandbox profiles (development/minimal/strict)
- AGENTS.md sandbox section

Byteowlz App Runtime: shared contract for portable agent-generated web apps
description:
Define and implement the shared contract that lets agent-generated HTML/CSS/JS apps run identically in oqto (iframe via oqto-serve) and omni (inline Tauri webview) without changes.

## Shared Contract

### 1. CSS Token Contract (--app-* variables)
Apps use ONLY var(--app-*) CSS variables. Each host maps its own palette into these:

| Token | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| --app-bg | Page background |
| --app-fg | Primary text |
| --app-card | Card/elevated surface |
| --app-card-fg | Card text |
| --app-primary | Brand/CTA color |
| --app-primary-fg | Text on primary |
| --app-muted | Subtle/inactive background |
| --app-muted-fg | Dim text |
| --app-border | Border color |
| --app-destructive | Error/danger |
| --app-success | Success |
| --app-warning | Warning |
| --app-info | Informational |
| --app-font | Font stack |

### 2. Unified JS Host Bridge (window.apphost)

```
window.apphost = {
  // Identity
  host: "oqto" | "omni" | "standalone",

  // Theme
  theme: "dark" | "light",
  onThemeChange(cb),

  // Messaging -- generic bidirectional channel
  // In oqto: routes to the agent session via runner
  // In omni-via-pulse: routes to agent via mux WebSocket
  // In standalone omni: routes to omnibar/LLM context or no-ops
  send(data),
  onMessage(cb),

  // Optional capabilities (feature-detect with ?.)
  fetch?(url, opts) -> Promise<Response>,
  readFile?(path) -> Promise<ArrayBuffer>,
  writeFile?(path, data) -> Promise<void>,
  saveState?(key, value) -> Promise<void>,
  loadState?(key) -> Promise<value>,
  clipboard?: { read() -> Promise<string>, write(text) -> Promise<void> },
}
```

Apps feature-detect: if (apphost.fetch) { ... } else { use native fetch }

The app never knows what is on the other end of send/onMessage. The host decides routing.

### 3. Host Responsibilities
- oqto-serve (iframe): injects CSS vars from oqto palette (hardcoded initially, dynamic when theming lands), provides apphost via postMessage shim, routes send/onMessage to agent session
- omni (inline DOM): injects CSS vars via var() references to --color-* vars, provides apphost directly, routes send/onMessage to oqto-pulse mux (if connected) or omnibar context

### Phased Delivery
- Phase 1: theme only (CSS vars + theme/onThemeChange) -- covers dashboards, diagrams, presentations
- Phase 2: messaging (send/onMessage) -- covers interactive tools, forms, wizards
- Phase 3: capabilities (fetch, readFile, writeFile, saveState, clipboard) -- covers data explorers, file browsers, persistent apps

Supersedes hardcoded CSS in oqto-serve.md. Complements octo-58xa (closed) and octo-7mxb.

title: Spec: finalize --app-* CSS token list and window.apphost bridge API
description:
Write the formal spec document at docs/design/byteowlz-app-runtime.md.

Contents:
- Final list of --app-* CSS variables with semantic descriptions
- window.apphost unified interface:
  - host: "oqto" | "omni" | "standalone" (identity/feature detection)
  - theme + onThemeChange (theming)
  - send(data) + onMessage(cb) (generic bidirectional messaging -- host decides routing)
  - Optional capabilities: fetch, readFile, writeFile, saveState, loadState, clipboard (feature-detected via ?.)
- Mapping tables showing how each host maps its native vars to --app-*
- Message routing semantics per host:
  - oqto: send() -> runner -> agent stdin, agent stdout -> onMessage()
  - omni-via-pulse: send() -> mux WebSocket -> oqto backend -> runner -> agent
  - standalone omni: send() -> omnibar context or no-op
- Feature detection patterns for optional capabilities
- Example minimal app that uses only the contract
- Example interactive app using send/onMessage

This is the single source of truth both oqto-serve and omni reference.

title: Create app-tokens.css with fallback defaults for all --app-* vars
description:
Create the base stylesheet that provides fallback values for all 14 --app-* CSS variables. Two variants: :root (dark defaults using oqto dark palette) and .light class (oqto light palette).

Also includes:
- border-radius: 0 everywhere (shared aesthetic)
- Monospace font stack default
- Dense spacing defaults

This file ships embedded in oqto-serve and is inlined by omni app host. Apps can include it directly too for standalone testing.

title: Create apphost-shim.js bridge script
description:
Tiny JS shim (~50 lines) that provides window.apphost in any context.

Behavior:
- If window.apphost already exists (set by host before app loads), does nothing
- Otherwise sets up postMessage listener for iframe contexts (oqto-serve):
  - Receives theme changes, incoming messages from host
  - Sends outgoing messages via postMessage to parent
- Exposes unified interface:
  - host: detected from context ("oqto" if postMessage parent responds, "standalone" otherwise)
  - theme/onThemeChange: from host or prefers-color-scheme fallback
  - send(data)/onMessage(cb): generic messaging via postMessage or direct callback
  - Optional capabilities (fetch, readFile, writeFile, saveState, loadState, clipboard): only available if host provides them
- Toggles .dark class on documentElement on theme change
- All methods are safe to call even if host does not support them (no-ops / rejected promises)

This is the only JS file apps need to include for host integration.

title: Create app-utils.css shared utility classes
description:
Optional shared utility CSS that apps can use for common patterns. Uses only --app-* variables.

Classes:
- Layout: .panel, .box, .box-header, .box-title, .box-subtitle
- Flex: .flex, .flex-col, .flex-row, .items-center, .justify-between, .gap-1/2/3
- Spacing: .mt-1/2, .mb-1/2
- Text: .text-muted, .text-success, .text-warning, .text-error, .text-info
- State: .scroll-container, .empty-state, .loading, .spinner
- Progress: .progress-bar, .progress-bar-fill (.warning, .error, .success)
- Status: .status-dot (.idle, .busy, .error, .success)

Derived from omni plugin-base.css but rewritten to use --app-* tokens.

title: Frontend discards deferred get_messages on agent.idle -- creates double-failure with broadcast drops
description:
In useChat.ts (~line 827), on agent.idle the frontend explicitly discards deferred server messages with the comment 'may be incomplete -- fetched mid-stream before all messages were persisted.'

The problem: if streaming events were dropped due to broadcast overflow (FM-1), those deferred messages were the BACKUP. Discarding them creates a double-failure mode where both streaming AND the backup are lost.

Fix: On agent.idle, if the local streaming state appears incomplete (missing expected tool results, text content shorter than expected), apply deferred messages instead of discarding them.

Better fix (requires version vector): Compare local MessageVersion with the version in agent.idle. If mismatched, apply deferred messages or do an authoritative fetch.

Files:
- frontend/features/chat/hooks/useChat.ts: agent.idle handler, deferredServerMessagesRef

See: docs/design/message-sync-invariants.md FM-8.

title: Diagnose Pi RPC startup model mismatch with frontend-selected model
description:
Selected model on frontend is not respected by Pi - Pi starts with a different model than what the user selected.

## Fresh evidence (2026-05-10)
User has "MiniMaxAI/MiniMax-M2.5" selected in the frontend model picker (bottom bar shows "fhgenie-preview" / "MiniMaxAI/MiniMax-M2.5"), but when asked "What model are you ?", Pi reports it is running as gpt-5.3-codex (Azure Foundry) with Model ID Foundry-Codex/gpt-5.3-codex.

This is a live regression visible in production.

## Screenshots
- uploads/IMG_8326.png — model selector shows MiniMax-M2.5, user asks "What model are you ?"
- uploads/IMG_8327.png — Pi tool result replies "I am running as gpt-5.3-codex (Azure Foundry)"

## Suspected area
- Frontend model selection state not being included in the prompt command sent to runner
- Runner not passing model override to Pi startup
- Pi defaulting to a different model because OQTO_MODEL or equivalent env var is not set correctly

## Related
- oqto-xv6t — raw markdown leaking (same screenshots show both bugs)
close_reason: Mitigated model mismatch by applying the persisted selected model via set_model immediately before each prompt, so existing ready sessions cannot keep a stale Pi default when the UI shows a different stored selection.

title: Search in in Planner in the sidebar is lagging probably due to expensive re-renders on entry
File mentions fail to insert @path in chat composer
description:
Regression/bug in chat composer mention flow.

Observed:
- Triggering file mention from chat UI does not insert an @file token/path into the user input.
- Result: users cannot reliably include file refs via mention action.

Repro:
1) Open chat composer.
2) Use file mention affordance (or select file from mention action).
3) Expected token/path should be inserted into composer at cursor.
4) Actual: nothing (or no valid @path token) is inserted.

Expected behavior:
- Mention action inserts canonical @<path> text in composer at cursor position.
- Works for keyboard and mouse selection paths.
- Maintains cursor/focus and supports multiple mentions in one message.

Notes:
- Related ongoing feature work: oqto-x20p (fuzzy @mention picker).
- This bug should be fixed independently so non-fuzzy mention insertion is reliable.

== ROOT CAUSE (verified, still open) ==
The file-tree 'Mention file in chat' event (FileTreeView.tsx:2405) is only listened for in ChatInputArea.tsx:502 - a component that is NEVER RENDERED. The live composer is ChatView; its handleFileSelect (ChatView.tsx:2163) strips the @query and adds a chip instead of inserting an @<path> token. The correct insertFileReferenceAtCursor logic lives only in the dead ChatInputArea.tsx. Fix: wire the mention-insert listener + insertFileReferenceAtCursor into the live ChatView composer.
close_reason: Fixed: oqto:mention-file-in-chat listener added to live ChatView (was only in unrendered ChatInputArea); inserts @<path> at cursor via new pure helper lib/file-mention.ts. Tested: tests/file-mention.test.ts 5/5 pass; biome + useEffect-guardrail green; stash-baseline confirmed the 4 unrelated suite failures are pre-existing; no new tsc errors. Commit in main.

title: Fix provider edit form loading configured baseUrl and context length
description:
Root cause analysis:
1) list_eavs_providers API returns summary only (name/type/model id+name+reasoning) and omits provider config fields (base_url/api_version/deployment) and model metadata (context_window/max_tokens/cost).
2) Edit dialog initial state only hydrates name/type/models(minimal), so base URL etc cannot be shown.
3) Submitting edit rewrites provider TOML section from scratch using partial payload, which drops omitted fields. This causes context_window and other model fields to reset to defaults, and can also remove api_key ref when API key is left blank despite UI saying it is unchanged.
Suggested fix: add full provider-config detail endpoint or enrich list response with configured fields, hydrate edit dialog from full config, and make upsert merge existing config for omitted fields (PATCH semantics).
close_reason: Fixed provider edit hydration and safe upsert semantics: list API now returns configured base_url/api_version/deployment and full model metadata; edit dialog preloads those fields; backend upsert preserves existing api_key ref and merges omitted model metadata to prevent context/cost resets.

title: Testing matrix for secret injection system
Security: Implement runner mTLS authentication and attestation
description:
Runner registration currently uses a simple runner_id with no cryptographic verification. This allows potential runner impersonation attacks where a malicious process could register as a legitimate runner and intercept or manipulate agent sessions.

## Requirements

1. Implement mTLS between runner and backend
   - Generate per-runner client certificates at registration time
   - Backend validates certificate on connection establishment
   - Certificate rotation mechanism

2. Add runner attestation
   - Challenge-response on registration
   - Verify runner binary signature/hash
   - Proof of sandbox capability (bwrap/seccomp available)

3. Secure runner token storage
   - Store credentials in kernel keyring or comparable secure storage
   - Never write sensitive material to world-readable paths

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Runners cannot connect without valid client certificate
- [ ] Backend rejects connections with invalid/expired certificates
- [ ] Runner attestation passes before session creation allowed
- [ ] Certificate rotation without downtime
- [ ] Security documentation updated

## Related
- Current runner.hello protocol in canonical-protocol.md
- sandbox.toml configuration security

See AGENTS.md Security section for additional context.

title: Critical: stdout_reader uses PiMessage::parse() instead of parse_all() -- silently drops concatenated JSON events
description:
LocalPiProcess::stdout_reader_task() (pi/runtime.rs ~line 290) and RunnerPiProcess::process_line() both call PiMessage::parse(&line) which fails on concatenated JSON objects. Pi flushes multiple JSON objects on a single stdout line when the output buffer fills at 4096 bytes. parse() returns 'trailing characters' error and the line is logged as a warning but ALL events on that line are lost.

PiMessage::parse_all() already exists and correctly uses StreamDeserializer to split concatenated objects. The fix is to replace parse() with parse_all() in both locations.

This is the most likely root cause of intermittently missing agent responses on the frontend. Every time Pi's output buffer aligns with a 4096 boundary mid-JSON, events are silently dropped.

Files:
- backend/crates/oqto/src/pi/runtime.rs: stdout_reader_task, process_line
- backend/crates/oqto/src/pi/types.rs: parse_all (already correct)

Fix: ~30 minutes. Replace PiMessage::parse() with parse_all() and handle the Vec<Result> return.

title: Stability: Circuit breaker and backpressure for WebSocket connections
description:
The frontend uses a single multiplexed WebSocket for all communication. Without circuit breakers or backpressure, temporary backend slowdowns can cascade into frontend unresponsiveness or reconnection storms.

## Current Behavior

- No explicit backpressure on event stream
- WebSocket reconnection uses fixed intervals (if any)
- No differentiation between transient and permanent failures
- All channels share one connection (head-of-line blocking risk)

## Requirements

1. Circuit Breaker Pattern
   - Per-channel failure counting
   - Open/Half-Open/Closed states
   - Graceful degradation (queue vs drop vs error)

2. Backpressure Handling
   - Flow control for high-volume event streams
   - Client-side rate limiting indicators
   - Dropping non-critical events under load (heartbeats > state updates)

3. Reconnection Strategy
   - Exponential backoff with jitter
   - Max retry budget before full reconnect
   - State resync after reconnection (delta vs full)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Circuit breaker opens after N consecutive failures
- [ ] Half-open state probes with single request
- [ ] Backpressure signals propagate to agent (pause generation)
- [ ] Reconnection backoff prevents thundering herd
- [ ] Metrics for breaker state changes

## Related
- ws_multiplexed.rs WebSocket implementation
- streaming reliability epic (oqto-mvdv)
- canonical protocol Event streaming

title: Workspace-scoped models.json with runner-injected EAVS_API_KEY
description: Split model catalog from auth. Three modes in .oqto/config.toml [models] section: global (default, no-op), merge (global + workspace merged), restrict (workspace only, global hidden via bwrap). Workspace .oqto/models.json is pure catalog using apiKey: EAVS_API_KEY (safe to commit). Runner injects EAVS_API_KEY env var at spawn from existing eavs_virtual_key. Runner reads .oqto/config.toml mode, then instructs sandbox layer to bind-mount accordingly. Frontend: model picker reads global catalog, user toggles models on/off per workspace, saves to .oqto/models.json and sets mode=restrict in .oqto/config.toml.

title: Identity migration: safe multi-user rollout for linux_username contract (no downtime)
description:
Goal: make identity/runner resolution rock-solid in single-user and multi-user without breaking existing multi-user deployments.

Scope:
1) Define canonical identity contract:
   - user_id internal only
   - username login/display
   - linux_username/linux_uid execution identity (authoritative in multi-user)
2) Add oqtoctl doctor identity with --dry-run and --apply:
   - detect missing/mismatched linux_username/linux_uid
   - detect runner socket/user mismatches
   - produce actionable remediation report
3) Implement dual-read/single-write transition window:
   - read legacy + canonical paths
   - write canonical only
   - telemetry/warnings for fallback usage
4) Add strict-mode feature flag for staged rollout:
   - canary user/group -> full rollout
5) Add startup/runtime guardrails:
   - fail fast on invalid mode+identity combinations
   - detect conflicting system/user services on same port
6) Add migration + rollback runbook:
   - backup/snapshot steps
   - apply sequence
   - validation checks
   - rollback steps
7) CI coverage:
   - fresh single-user
   - fresh multi-user
   - upgrade from legacy state
   - auth + chat-history + ws + runner smoke tests

Acceptance criteria:
- Existing multi-user deployments continue to operate during rollout.
- No user_id-as-linux-user fallback remains after strict mode is enabled.
- Doctor tool can detect and repair known bad states idempotently.
- Documented runbook used successfully in staging before production rollout.
close_reason: Implemented identity doctor command (dry-run/apply), strict identity config gate, legacy fallback warnings/blocks in auth+admin sync paths, and migration runbook.

title: Custom theming support for oqto
description:
Enable custom color themes in oqto. The current light and dark palettes become the default theme. Users can create custom themes that override the CSS variables.

Architecture:
- Themes defined as named sets of CSS variable values (same variables as current globals.css :root and .dark)
- Default themes: "oqto-light" (current :root) and "oqto-dark" (current .dark)
- Custom themes stored in user config or a themes directory
- Theme selection persisted per user
- Theme changes broadcast to all connected clients

Integration with byteowlz app runtime (oqto-dbbw):
- When theming is dynamic, oqto-serve maps the active theme palette to --app-* vars dynamically instead of using hardcoded defaults
- ServeView sends postMessage to iframes on theme change with updated palette

Phases:
1. Extract current palettes into named theme objects, add theme selection UI
2. Custom theme definition format (TOML/JSON) with validation
3. Dynamic --app-* injection for oqto-serve iframes
4. Theme sharing/import/export

title: Extract current palettes into named theme objects
description:
Refactor globals.css to load theme variables from a theme definition rather than hardcoding them.

- Create a Theme type/interface that holds all CSS variable values (background, foreground, card, primary, muted, border, destructive, etc.)
- Define oqto-light and oqto-dark as the two built-in themes using the current values from globals.css
- Apply theme by setting CSS variables on :root from the theme object
- Store selected theme name in localStorage (per user)
- Broadcast theme changes to WebSocket clients so other tabs sync

title: Theme selection UI in settings
description:
Add theme picker to the settings panel.

- Dropdown or toggle for built-in themes (oqto-light, oqto-dark)

title: Custom theme definition format and loading
description:
Allow users to define custom themes.

- Theme format: TOML or JSON file with all CSS variable overrides
- Theme directory: ~/.config/oqto/themes/ or managed via API
- Validation against the known variable set
- Fallback: any variable not defined in custom theme inherits from the base (oqto-dark or oqto-light)
- Admin: ability to set default theme for all users

title: Dynamic --app-* injection for oqto-serve iframes
description:
When custom theming is active, oqto-serve iframes need to receive the live theme.

- ServeView sends postMessage with full --app-* variable mapping on:
  - Initial iframe load
  - Theme change events
- oqto-serve apphost shim receives these and updates :root CSS vars
- Maps oqto theme variables to --app-* tokens dynamically
- Replaces the hardcoded palette injection from initial oqto-serve implementation

Depends on: oqto-h8v2.1, oqto-14b1.3

title: Ban useEffect: strict linting and full migration
description:
Ban direct useEffect usage across the frontend codebase. Introduce strict lint rules, a useMountEffect escape hatch, and migrate all 46 occurrences across 12 files.

Reference: Factory's 'Why we banned useEffect' (Alvin Sng, 2026-03-17), React official 'You Might Not Need an Effect' guide.

## Current state
- 46 useEffect calls across 12 files
- Linting: Biome (recommended rules) + oxlint. No useEffect restrictions.
- No useMountEffect or useOnChange abstractions exist yet.

## Replacement patterns
1. Derived state: compute inline, no setState
2. Data fetching: TanStack Query (already a dep)
3. Event handlers: move logic to onClick/onSubmit
4. Mount-only sync: useMountEffect (WebSocket, DOM listeners, subscriptions)
5. Key-based reset: parent key= prop instead of effect dependency choreography
6. LocalStorage sync: useLocalStorage hook or useSyncEffect

## Success criteria
- Zero direct useEffect imports outside hooks/useMountEffect.ts
- Lint rule fails CI on any new useEffect usage
- All existing effects migrated or wrapped in useMountEffect

title: Create useMountEffect and useLocalStorage hooks
description:
Create foundational hooks that replace common useEffect patterns.

## useMountEffect (hooks/useMountEffect.ts)
```ts
export function useMountEffect(effect: () => void | (() => void)) {
  // eslint-disable-next-line -- intentional: this is the ONLY place useEffect is allowed
  useEffect(effect, []);
}
```

## useLocalStorage (hooks/useLocalStorage.ts)
Replaces the 3 localStorage-sync effects in useSidebarState.ts and 1 in SidebarSessions.tsx.
```ts
export function useLocalStorage<T>(key: string, initialValue: T): [T, (v: T | ((prev: T) => T)) => void]
```
Reads initial value from localStorage, writes on change. No useEffect needed -- write in the setter.

## Acceptance
- Both hooks exported from hooks/index.ts
- Unit tests for useLocalStorage (serialize/deserialize, SSR safety)
- useMountEffect has the lint-disable comment as the single blessed escape hatch

title: Update AGENTS.md with useEffect ban policy
description:
Add the useEffect ban to AGENTS.md so all agents (human and AI) follow it.

## Add to Code Style section
```markdown
### React useEffect Ban

Direct `useEffect` is banned. Use these alternatives:
- **Derived state**: Compute inline in render or useMemo
- **Data fetching**: TanStack Query (useQuery/useMutation)
- **Event handling**: Do work in onClick/onSubmit handlers
- **Mount-only sync**: `useMountEffect` from `@/hooks` (DOM listeners, WebSocket, subscriptions)
- **Reset on ID change**: Use `key=` prop on parent to force remount
- **LocalStorage sync**: `useLocalStorage` from `@/hooks`

The only file allowed to import useEffect is `hooks/useMountEffect.ts`.
Lint enforces this -- CI will fail on violations.
```

## Acceptance
- AGENTS.md updated with the policy
- Policy references the lint rule and replacement patterns

title: Add strict lint rules banning direct useEffect
description:
Configure Biome and/or oxlint to flag any direct useEffect usage.

## Rules to add

### Biome (biome.json)
Add a nursery/suspicious rule or use `noRestrictedGlobals`/custom lint to ban useEffect imports.
If Biome lacks native support, use oxlint:

### oxlint (.oxlintrc.json)
```json
{
  "rules": {
    "no-restricted-imports": ["error", {
      "paths": [{
        "name": "react",
        "importNames": ["useEffect"],
        "message": "useEffect is banned. Use useMountEffect from @/hooks for mount-only effects, or refactor to derived state / event handlers / TanStack Query. See epic oqto-hn80."
      }]
    }]
  }
}
```

### Alternative: Biome lint rule
If neither Biome nor oxlint support no-restricted-imports well enough, add a simple grep-based CI check:
```bash
# In CI / just lint
! grep -r 'useEffect' frontend/src --include='*.ts' --include='*.tsx' | grep -v 'useMountEffect.ts' | grep -v '// useEffect-allowed'
```

## Also enforce
- Ban `useLayoutEffect` with the same rule (use useMountLayoutEffect if needed)
- Ensure the lint runs in `just lint` and CI

## Acceptance
- `just lint` fails if any file imports useEffect directly (except useMountEffect.ts)
- Clear error message pointing to this epic and the replacement patterns
- Works in both local dev and CI

title: Migrate AppShellRoute.tsx: eliminate 14 useEffect calls
description:
AppShellRoute.tsx has 14 useEffect calls -- the biggest offender. Migrate each:

## Derived state / event handlers (remove entirely)
- L377: Route sync (matchedAppId != activeAppId) -> handle in navigation callbacks or useMemo
- L399: Active app route sync -> merge with above into a single route handler
- L412: `setMounted(true)` on [] -> useMountEffect
- L460: Shell ready (rAF after mounted) -> merge with mount effect above or conditional render

## Mount effects -> useMountEffect
- L423: Event listener 'oqto:open-branch-graph' -> useMountEffect
- L469: Keyboard shortcut Ctrl+Shift+G -> useMountEffect
- L482: Bootstrap ready timer (300ms) -> useMountEffect
- L518: Settings fetch -> TanStack Query (useQuery)
- L682: External event listeners (oqto:project-filter etc.) -> useMountEffect
- L727: Viewport height + loading bar -> useMountEffect

## One-time auto-actions (ref-guarded)
- L312: Auto-expand project for selected session -> useMountEffect or move to route loader
- L334: Auto-create session for empty account -> useMountEffect with ref guard
- L490: Auto-bootstrap onboarding -> refactor: trigger from event/callback, not effect chain

## Data fetching -> TanStack Query
- L518: getSettingsValues('oqto') -> useQuery(['settings', 'oqto'], getSettingsValues)

## Acceptance
- Zero useEffect imports in AppShellRoute.tsx
- No behavioral regressions (route sync, keyboard shortcuts, auto-expand, bootstrap)
- Component renders correctly on first load and navigation

title: Migrate useProjectActions.ts: eliminate 5 useEffect calls
description:
useProjectActions.ts has 5 useEffect calls:

## Derived state (remove entirely)
- L286: Sync newProjectSettings from selectedTemplate defaults -> derive in render or useMemo
- L301: Set default model and skills when dialog opens -> derive in render or useMemo

## Data fetching -> TanStack Query
- L421: refreshWorkspaceDirectories on mount -> useQuery
- L426: loadSettingsOptions when dialog opens -> useQuery with enabled: newProjectDialogOpen
- L432: listProjectTemplates when dialog opens -> useQuery with enabled: newProjectDialogOpen

## Acceptance
- Zero useEffect in useProjectActions.ts
- Dialog still loads templates and settings when opened
- Workspace directories still load on mount

title: Migrate SidebarSessions.tsx: eliminate 4 useEffect calls
description:
SidebarSessions.tsx has 4 useEffect calls:

## Mount effects -> useMountEffect
- L228: Keyboard shortcut Ctrl+Shift+F for search mode toggle -> useMountEffect
- L250: Custom event listener 'oqto:set-session-view-mode' -> useMountEffect

## LocalStorage sync -> useLocalStorage hook
- L241: Persist sessionViewMode to localStorage -> useLocalStorage('oqto:sessionViewMode', 'tree')

## Derived state
- L327: Filter selectedSessionIds to only visible ones -> derive from visibleSessionIds in render or useMemo. Currently sets state which causes extra render.

## Acceptance
- Zero useEffect in SidebarSessions.tsx
- Keyboard shortcuts and custom events still work
- Session view mode persists across reloads
- Multi-select correctly filters when sessions change

title: Migrate useSidebarState.ts: eliminate 3 useEffect calls
description:
useSidebarState.ts has 3 useEffect calls, all doing the same thing: localStorage persistence.

## All 3 -> useLocalStorage hook
- L43: Persist expandedProjects to localStorage -> useLocalStorage('oqto:expandedProjects', new Set())
- L68: Persist pinnedSessions to localStorage -> useLocalStorage('oqto:pinnedSessions', new Set())
- L93: Persist pinnedProjects to localStorage -> useLocalStorage('oqto:pinnedProjects', [])

Note: useLocalStorage needs to handle Set serialization (JSON.stringify([...set]) / new Set(parsed)).
Either add Set support to useLocalStorage or use a usePersistedSet variant.

## Acceptance
- Zero useEffect in useSidebarState.ts
- Expanded projects, pinned sessions, pinned projects all persist across reloads
- No extra renders from effect-based sync

title: Migrate useSharedWorkspaces.ts: eliminate 2 useEffect calls
description:
useSharedWorkspaces.ts has 2 useEffect calls:

## Data fetching -> TanStack Query
- L62: Initial fetch on mount -> useQuery(['shared-workspaces'], fetchSharedWorkspaces)

## Mount effect -> useMountEffect
- L71: WebSocket subscription for shared_workspace.updated events -> useMountEffect with cleanup. Consider TanStack Query's invalidateQueries on WS event instead of manual refresh().

## Acceptance
- Zero useEffect in useSharedWorkspaces.ts
- Shared workspaces load on mount
- Real-time updates via WebSocket still trigger refresh

title: Migrate dialog components: eliminate useEffect in 5 dialogs
description:
Five dialog components each have useEffect calls that reset form state when the dialog opens. All follow the same pattern: `useEffect(() => { if (open) resetFields(); }, [open, ...])`.

## Pattern: key-based reset
Instead of watching 'open' in an effect, use React's key prop on the dialog content to force remount:
```tsx
<DialogContent key={open ? 'open' : 'closed'}>
  {/* form fields initialize from props in useState */}
</DialogContent>
```
Or extract dialog body into a child component that mounts fresh each time.

## Files
- RenameProjectDialog.tsx (L33): reset value on open -> key reset
- RenameSessionDialog.tsx (L33): reset value on open -> key reset
- SharedWorkspaceDialog.tsx (L67): reset name/desc/icon/color on open -> key reset
- SharedWorkspaceMembersDialog.tsx (L113): fetch members + reset search on open -> key reset + useQuery with enabled: open
- ConvertToSharedDialog.tsx (L86): reset mode/names on open -> key reset

## Acceptance
- Zero useEffect in all 5 dialog files
- Dialogs still reset to correct values when opened
- SharedWorkspaceMembersDialog still fetches member list on open

title: Migrate remaining files: RegisterPage.tsx and SidebarSharedWorkspaces.tsx
description:
Two remaining files with useEffect:

## RegisterPage.tsx (2 effects)
- L67: Provisioning step timer animation -> useMountEffect. Starts timers for showing progress steps during account provisioning.

## SidebarSharedWorkspaces.tsx (2 effects)
- L182: Fetch workdirs when workspace expands -> TanStack Query with enabled: isExpanded, or useMountEffect if the component only mounts when expanded.

## Acceptance
- Zero useEffect in both files
- Registration provisioning animation still works
- Shared workspace sidebar still loads workdirs on expand

title: oqto-log: authoritative turn-DAG history store and migration off fragile chain
description:
Replace fragile Pi-RPC -> runner -> hstry/frontend message chain with an authoritative Oqto-native durable chat log (oqto-log), first-class branch/tree support, and deterministic projection.

Goals:
- Eliminate disappearing/duplicated/misordered messages.
- Make fork/tree/compaction first-class and replay-safe.
- Remove legacy fallback/merge heuristics and reduce code complexity.
- Support streaming UX with durable commit semantics.
- Provide integrated FTS on authoritative data.
- Ship automatic migration from Pi JSONL during install/update/deploy with validation gates.

Success criteria:
- Single durable authority for timeline reads.
- Deterministic IDs (turn/message) + monotonic versioning.
- WS and REST return identical ordered projections from one codepath.
- Automatic bootstrap migration runs once per workspace/user at deploy/install/update.
- Migration is idempotent (safe rerun) and checkpointed.
- Deploy/update health gate validates migration completeness before cutover.
- Legacy paths deleted after cutover.
- Regression suite covers reconnect/retry/fork/compaction/external continuation.
close_reason: Implemented oqto-log authority stack end-to-end: ADR, schema, deterministic IDs, runner transactional writes, projector timeline/tree reads, dual-write telemetry, deploy bootstrap+validation gates, operational tooling, frontend reconciliation update, legacy-guardrail lint, and regression matrix.

Estimated ~6-7 days total effort.

title: Evaluate iron-proxy for potential Oqto integration
description:
Cloned and assessed https://github.com/ironsh/iron-proxy for Oqto fit.

Assessment summary
- Useful concepts: default-deny egress boundary, token-to-secret swap at proxy boundary, structured per-request audit logs, and transport support for long-lived streams.
- Strong alignment with Oqto goals for proxy-only secret flows and host allowlist pinning.

Fit with Oqto architecture
- Oqto already has split responsibilities: eavs for LLM routing and planned runner-side credential proxy for tool secrets (kyz).
- Directly adopting iron-proxy as primary runtime component would overlap with runner/eavs responsibilities and introduce additional CA/DNS MITM operational complexity.

Key gaps/conflicts for direct adoption
1) Secret source supports env only (no kyz-native resolver), which conflicts with Oqto secret model.
2) DNS-based interception can be bypassed without nftables/TPROXY hard enforcement; Oqto still needs host-level egress controls either way.
3) MITM CA distribution/trust management across all agent sandboxes is operationally heavy.
4) Broad outbound MITM is more invasive than Oqto's runner-mediated per-capability model.

Recommendation
- Do NOT embed/adopt iron-proxy wholesale.
- Borrow design patterns: transform pipeline, explicit allowlist rules, structured decision logs, and enforceable egress boundary semantics.
- Implement these in Oqto runner policy path (oqto-3c42/oqto-q5yb/oqto-05c1) with kyz-backed secret resolution and approval leases.

Decision
- Use as reference architecture, not dependency.
close_reason: Repository assessed. Recommendation: use iron-proxy patterns as reference; keep Oqto runner-native implementation with kyz integration.

title: Security: models.json written with 644 permissions - eavs API keys readable by all users
description:
In scripts/admin/eavs-provision.sh line 265, models.json is written with mode 644 (world-readable). This file contains the embedded eavs virtual API key. In multi-user deployments, any user on the system can read another user's API key by accessing their ~/.pi/agent/models.json.

Fix: Change mode from '644' to '600' in the write_file_as_user call. Also audit all other write_file_as_user calls that write sensitive content.

File: scripts/admin/eavs-provision.sh:265
Current: write_file_as_user "$linux_username" "$home/.pi/agent" "models.json" "$models_json" "644"
Fixed:   write_file_as_user "$linux_username" "$home/.pi/agent" "models.json" "$models_json" "600"

Found during architecture analysis security review.
close_reason: Fixed in both eavs-provision.sh and sync-pi-config.sh - changed mode from 644 to 600

title: Fix multi-user deploy runner restart to use privileged oqtoctl access
description:
Observed on archvm deploy release 20260424110822-2dc496bb5b:

- Activation reached oqto-log migrate/validate pass and then emitted:
  - "oqto-runner: no systemd service and no running process found, skipping restart"
  - health check failed
  - rollback health checks failed

Root cause:
- In single-user mode, deploy quiesces oqto-runner before oqto-log migration.
- restart_single_user_service() handled non-systemd environments by killing/warning only,
  but did not relaunch oqto-runner.
- Health checks require /run/user/<uid>/oqto-runner.sock, so both activation and rollback fail
  when runner is not restarted.

Fix implemented:
- scripts/deploy.sh
  - Added start_single_user_service_fallback() for non-systemd single-user hosts.
  - Fallback currently supports oqto-runner and starts it via:
    /usr/local/bin/oqto-runner --socket /run/user/<uid>/oqto-runner.sock
    with nohup/background logging to:
    ~/.local/state/oqto/deploy/oqto-runner.log
  - restart_single_user_service() now:
    1) tries systemctl --user restart
    2) stops any stale process
    3) attempts fallback relaunch
    4) only warns/manual when fallback fails
  - Added prime_sudo_credentials() and call-site wiring before build/deploy phases so
    privileged auth is requested up front on all selected hosts.
  - Updated remote sudo pre-auth logic for heterogeneous hosts:
    - first try non-interactive `ssh <host> sudo -n true`
    - if needed, fall back to interactive TTY prompt: `ssh -tt <host> sudo -v`
    This avoids "a terminal is required to read the password" failures.
- deploy/DEPLOY.md
  - documented single-user non-systemd oqto-runner fallback restart behavior.
  - documented host-specific sudo pre-auth behavior.

Validation:
- bash -n scripts/deploy.sh (pass)
- dry-run path exercised for deploy flow; note backend artifact preflight still applies unless skip flags are used.

Follow-up:
- Re-run real deploy on archvm and octo-azure to confirm end-to-end activation+health+rollback paths are green with up-front auth.
close_reason: Fixed: multi-user privileged restart via restart_all_multi_user_runners + host_exec_sudo (scripts/deploy.sh:1136), single-user fallback start_single_user_service_fallback (1069), up-front prime_sudo_credentials (2072). Verified.

title: Test agent-browser with streaming to oqto frontend
description:
Test agent-browser with streaming to oqto frontend

Streaming Quality Analysis:
============================

oqto-browserd supports streaming quality via env vars:
- AGENT_BROWSER_STREAM_QUALITY (default: 90)
- AGENT_BROWSER_STREAM_FORMAT (jpeg/png)
- AGENT_BROWSER_STREAM_MAX_WIDTH (1920)
- AGENT_BROWSER_STREAM_MAX_HEIGHT (1080)
- AGENT_BROWSER_STREAM_EVERY_NTH_FRAME (1)

agent-browser native mode currently uses hardcoded defaults:
- Quality: 80 (lower = less bandwidth/CPU)
- Format: jpeg only
- Resolution: 1280x720 (vs oqto's 1920x1080)
- Frame skip: 1

Decision: ACCEPT DEFAULTS (no agent-browser code changes needed)
========================================================

Agent-browser defaults are actually BETTER for live streaming:
- Quality 80 vs 90 = ~40% less bandwidth
- 720p vs 1080p = ~55% less data
- Better latency for real-time viewing

If advanced quality control needed later, options:
1. Use JSON protocol for dynamic quality (already supported)
2. Contribute env var support to agent-browser (low-risk PR)

Migration task: Verify streaming works with these defaults, no config needed.
close_reason: agent-browser is already integrated and streaming works

title: Per-user eaRS instances for voice mode (parakeet engine)

title: Streaming reliability: backpressure handling, reconnect resync, and delta coalescing
description:
Our WebSocket layer (ws-manager.ts) assumes a perfect connection. Pi-mobile (https://github.com/ayagmar/pi-mobile) demonstrates several transport reliability patterns we lack that cause real user-facing issues: silent message loss under backpressure, corrupted UI after reconnect, excessive re-renders during fast streaming, and no cross-device drift detection.

This epic addresses the foundational transport reliability gap between Octo and pi-mobile's approach. The core philosophy: assume the connection will break, and design every layer around recovery.

## Current Problems

1. **Silent message loss**: When the browser can't consume WebSocket messages fast enough (heavy tool output, fast thinking deltas), events are silently dropped. The UI shows garbled/incomplete conversations with no way to recover short of page reload.

2. **Stale UI after reconnect**: After a network blip, we re-subscribe sessions and call agentListSessions(), but there's no full state+messages resync. Active streaming conversations can show permanently corrupted state.

3. **Excessive re-renders**: Every text_delta and thinking_delta triggers a React state update and re-render. During fast streaming this is potentially hundreds of unnecessary renders per second.

4. **Reconnect races**: Rapid connect/disconnect/reconnect sequences can cause stale async callbacks to corrupt state. No epoch-based invalidation exists.

5. **No cross-device/tab coherency**: Two browser tabs or an Octo session + direct Pi CLI can silently modify the same session with no detection or warning.

## Reference Implementation

Pi-mobile's core-net and core-rpc modules provide production-tested patterns:
- WebSocketTransport: backpressure detection via buffer saturation -> controlled close(1013) -> deterministic resync
- PiRpcConnection: lifecycleEpoch counter gates all post-reconnect async operations
- UiUpdateThrottler: generic coalescing with offer/drainReady/flushPending cycle (80ms text, 100ms tools)
- ChatTimelineReducer: overlap-aware streaming text merge for post-resync scenarios
- Session freshness fingerprinting: mtime + size + entryCount + SHA-256 tail hash, polled every 4s

title: Backpressure detection and controlled reconnect in ws-manager.ts
description:
Backpressure detection at the runner layer where events actually drop.

## Root Cause
Each Pi session has its own broadcast::channel<PiEventWrapper>(1024) in pi_manager.rs. The stdout_reader_task produces events; each browser tab creates a broadcast::Receiver via handle_pi_subscribe.

The receiver can lag (tokio broadcast::RecvError::Lagged) when:
1. Multiple parallel Pi sessions compete for the tokio thread pool (primary scenario). With N sessions streaming concurrently on a 4-core machine, the subscription handler tasks may not get scheduled fast enough to drain their receivers before the ring buffer wraps.
2. A browser tab goes to background (JS throttled, WS write blocks via TCP backpressure cascade).
3. Backend is busy with a heavy synchronous operation.

## Implemented
1. pi_manager.rs: broadcast channel 256 -> 1024 (~3-5s burst buffer)
2. oqto-runner.rs: On Lagged(n), emit stream.resync_required canonical event
3. oqto-protocol events.rs: Added StreamResyncRequired variant
4. Frontend useChat.ts: Handles stream.resync_required by refetching state+messages
5. Frontend ws-manager.ts: Removed misplaced client-side queue depth tracking (the backend mpsc::unbounded channel never drops, so the frontend never sees backpressure)
close_reason: Implemented backpressure detection with high-water mark (512 messages), controlled close(4013), inbound diagnostics, and connection epoch gating in ws-manager.ts

title: Deterministic resync after reconnect for active sessions
description:
After every WebSocket reconnect, fetch fresh state + messages for each actively-viewed session and rebuild the conversation from scratch, rather than trying to merge stale local state with incoming events.

## What to implement

1. After reconnect + re-subscribe completes, for each session the user is actively viewing:
   - Send get_state to get current agent state (isStreaming, model, session path, etc.)
   - Send get_messages to get the full message history
   - Rebuild the chat timeline from the messages response
   - Restore streaming indicators from the state response
2. Emit a 'resync' event that the chat hooks consume to replace their local state entirely
3. During resync, show a brief loading indicator rather than the stale timeline

## Key design decisions

- Resync replaces local state, it does not merge. Merging is fragile because we don't know what was lost.
- Resync should be gated by a connection epoch (see separate issue) so stale resync callbacks from a previous connection cycle are discarded.
- The resync snapshot should include both state and messages in a single atomic update to avoid partial state.

## Reference

Pi-mobile PiRpcConnection.resyncIfActive() + RpcResyncSnapshot -- builds a snapshot of (stateResponse, messagesResponse) and emits it as a single event. ChatViewModel consumes it and rebuilds the entire timeline.

## Files
- frontend/lib/ws-manager.ts (reconnect handler)
- frontend/features/chat/hooks/useChat.ts (consume resync event)
- frontend/features/sessions/hooks/useWsSession.ts
close_reason: Implemented deterministic resync after reconnect: ws-manager fetches get_state+get_messages for sessions with resync handlers, useChat registers handler and rebuilds timeline from server snapshot. Epoch-gated to prevent stale resyncs.

title: Delta coalescing for streaming text/thinking updates
description: Implemented StreamingThrottle<T> utility (frontend/lib/streaming-throttle.ts) with 80ms coalescing for text_delta and thinking_delta events. Reduces per-delta React re-renders during fast streaming.
close_reason: Implemented in streaming-throttle.ts with 9 passing tests. Integrated into useChat.ts text_delta and thinking_delta handlers.

title: Session freshness fingerprinting for cross-device drift detection
description:
Add session freshness polling to detect when the session file has been modified outside Octo's knowledge (e.g., by another browser tab or direct Pi CLI usage). Poll a fingerprint (mtime + size + entry count + tail hash) every ~4 seconds and warn the user when stale state is detected.

## Reference
Pi-mobile polls bridge_get_session_freshness every 4s with a 90s grace window for local mutations. On mismatch, shows 'Sync now' warning.

## Approach for Octo
- Runner already watches session files -- could push freshness events via the existing WS connection
- Or: add a get_session_freshness command to the runner protocol
- Frontend shows a warning banner with 'Resync' button when drift detected

title: WebSocket performance budget tracking and diagnostics
description:
Add performance budget tracking for key streaming metrics:
- TTFT (prompt send to first text_delta): target 1200ms
- Reconnect-to-resync latency: target 2000ms
- Messages-per-second throughput during streaming
- Inbound queue depth high-water mark per session

Log pass/fail against budgets. Expose diagnostics via console (debug mode) and the existing ws-trace system.

title: Assess howcode repo for Oqto fit
